video_title: Why Puerto Rico’s Economy Doesn’t Work
transcript: In 1980, Puerto Rico was home to 3.2 million people, about the same as South Carolina. But whereas South Carolina gained another 2 million residents over the next 50 years, Puerto Rico gained just 2,000 people, 2,705 to be exact, a net increase of 0.08% or 61 people a year. In that time, the island experienced a spectacular rise, followed by a spectacular and unprecedented fall. It went from surpassing the economic growth of the U.S. mainland during the Reagan and .com booms to a 15-year period of negative growth dubbed a death spiral by its former governor. So how did Puerto Rico's economic future become so bleak, so fast? Concert by Nebula. Watch my exclusive series China Actually and dozens more for just $2.50 per month with the link in the description. By the late 1940s, Puerto Rico had been a U.S. territory for nearly half a century. Unlike the rest of the American economy, however, it remained almost entirely dominated by agriculture. Pressure began growing to close this gap, to develop the island into something more than a giant sugar cane plantation, particularly during the Cold War. With Communist Cuba less than 500 miles away, this tiny appendage of the U.S. empire took on ideological and geopolitical significance. Thus came Operation Bootstrap, a multi-decade campaign to transform Puerto Rico into a modern, industrial, export-oriented economy in the style of Hong Kong or South Korea. An component of which was something called Section 936 of the U.S. tax code. Section 936 lowered the federal income tax to zero on money earned in Puerto Rico. This at the time was revolutionary. U.S. corporations were then only flirting with the idea of outsourcing. Microsoft, Nike, and Pepsi hadn't yet left the states for Taiwan, Malaysia, and China. Puerto Rico gave them a chance to experiment. Here they got access to cheap labor, generous tax breaks, and pristine white sand beaches, all within the American Empire. But this was really, really lucrative for one industry in particular, Big Pharma. Under Section 936, of course, the goal was to claim as much of your revenue as possible in Puerto Rico, making it tax-free. This was ideal for pharmaceutical companies because the price of a drug is almost entirely attributable to its patents. And as abstract legal property, patents can be instantly moved anywhere on the planet. A U.S. parent company like Pfizer could transfer its intellectual property to its Puerto Rican subsidiary. Then, when someone in Charlottesville or San Jose bought some libator, it could argue those patents were responsible for, say, 90% of the sale price, shifting that income to the tropics. At the same time, you had to prove some tangible link between the island and the product itself. This too was perfect. After all, once a drug is developed, tested, and brought to market, producing the two millionth tablet is cheap and can be done just about anywhere. By 1990, 17 of the top 21 most popular drugs in the United States were made in Puerto Rico. One factory single-handedly produced all of North America's Viagra. And by one, as new blockbuster drugs exploded onto the U.S. market, the island prospered. Manufacturing soon accounted for nearly 40% of its GDP, twice the average in the region. There was just one problem. It all depended on the whims of Congress. Section 936 was signed into law with a stroke of a pen, and it could just as easily be taken away. And by the mid-90s, the Cold War was over, and Puerto Rico had lost its strategic importance. Meanwhile, America's focus turned toward tax reform and reducing the deficit. Besides, if the goal was to subsidize the development of its distant territories, Congress realized, this was a wildly inefficient way to do so. For every one employee they hired in Puerto Rico, for example, pharmaceutical companies were saving $70,000 in taxes. In other words, the U.S. government was effectively paying $70,000 to employ a single person. In a place where the per capita GDP was about $9,000. Section 936 began looking more and more like a giant wasteful handout to big forma. But rather than reform the law, perhaps by creating new incentives for corporations to truly invest in the Puerto Rican economy, Congress simply deleted Section 936 from the tax code and quickly moved on, leaving Puerto Rico in the dust. That was bad enough, but things soon got worse. Previously, Puerto Rico held a privileged position when it came to trade. Unlike all other U.S. territories, Guam, American Samoa, the U.S. Virgin Islands, and the Northern Mariana Islands, Puerto Rico is located within the U.S. customs area, meaning drugs manufactured on the island and shipped to the American mainland weren't subject to tariffs or trade restrictions, while those in, say, the Bahamas or Trinidad or Colombia were. But as free trade became the norm with the passage of NAFTA, China's joining of the WTO and dozens of other bilateral trade agreements, Puerto Rico became just like the rest of them. So, companies left for Ireland, Mexico, China, and elsewhere. Under Section 936 was fully phased out in 2006, the Puerto Rican economy plunged into free fall. And to make matters even worse, the Great Recession came two years later. By 2013, 230,000 out of Puerto Rico's 1.2 million total jobs had permanently disappeared. At this point, the island had been losing revenue for decades as companies moved elsewhere. At the same time, the government had accumulated a large and growing bureaucracy. Even between 1960 and 80 alone, the number of public employees had already tripled. Facing shrinking revenue and rising expenses, something had to change. Now 49 out of the 50 states are legally required to balance their budgets, spending no more than they earn. Of course, cities and states can and do borrow money. When the city of Binghamton, New York decides it needs a new freeway, it can ask for a loan by selling municipal bonds. But when those investors ask how it intends to repay them, it can't shrug its shoulders and say, we'll figure that out later. It needs a plan. And the same is true of Puerto Rico. Its constitution states that appropriations shall not exceed total revenues. At least, that's what it says in English. The problem is that when written in Spanish, the language widely spoken on the island, the word revenues in the phrase total revenues was mistranslated as resources. And in 1974, it decided that those resources included bonds. Meaning, the Commonwealth of Puerto Rico can only spend as much as it earns. Unless it takes on debt, in which case, it can spend as much as it wants with no real plan for repayment. So much for a balanced budget. This loophole proved far too tempting to resist. The alternative, after all, was initiating layoffs, and politicians who used the L word tend to lose their re-elections. Now, it would be one thing if the government used this creative interpretation of its constitution to invest in its future. If it borrowed money to build a new highway to lower transportation costs and thus attract manufacturing companies to return to the island, that might be a good use of the loophole. But these were not one-off revenue-generating projects. The government was borrowing money for regular ongoing expenses, to pay the salaries of the firefighters and teachers and garbage collectors it had too many of. For long, it was taking on new loans to pay back the old ones, sinking further and further into a giant hole of debt. A U.S. government report found that 16 out of 20 bonds issued between 2000 and 2017 were used exclusively to repay or refinance existing loans. In 2006, Puerto Rico even introduced a new sales tax for this very purpose. Effectively making ordinary citizens paid directly for the mistakes its politicians made years ago, all while scaring away companies and reducing economic activity. By 2015, it had one of the highest sales taxes in the United States at 11.5%, despite nearly half of its residents living below the poverty line. Normally, this would all end fairly quickly. You can't keep borrowing money if no one is willing to lend it, and who would keep lending money to someone who so clearly can't repay you. Normally, but Puerto Rico was no normal borrower. First, its constitution states that bondholders are prioritized in case of default, meaning if the government ran out of money, lenders would get paid first, before, for example, teachers and doctors got their salaries.

video_title: The Making of the Modern Middle East
transcript: Thread all of our lifetimes. The Middle East is a region that has been nearly synonymous with warfare, instability, and conflict. You have heard nearly every single year of your life about some kind of conflict going on in this place, regardless of when you were born, because conflicts have been raging here without a pause for nearly the entirety of the past century. Just since the turn of the 21st century alone, there have been major wars fought across the territories of Iraq, Syria, Lebanon, Israel, Palestine, and Yemen that have variously attracted the military involvements of Iran, Jordan, Saudi Arabia, Egypt, the United Arab Emirates, Turkey, and powers from even beyond the Middle East like the United States, United Kingdom, France, and Russia. These 21st century conflicts in the Middle East alone have claimed the lives of millions of people, and forced millions more into exile and refugee status while costing trillions of dollars, making the Middle East arguably the most geopolitically turbulent region anywhere in the 21st century world. But why is this part of the world the way that it is? Why do you always hear about wars and conflicts taking place here? And why will you probably continue hearing about wars and conflicts taking place here for the rest of your life? The answers to those questions are complex, because the modern Middle East is the most geopolitically complicated chessboard in the world to really understand. With dozens of different actors and competing interests all variously working against each other at some times and then working together at others. But in order to understand the root cause of the modern Middle East's many problems, you only have to rewind the clock a little more than a hundred years ago to learn how the Middle East borders we know today came into being. And why? They have guaranteed more than a centuries worth of forever wars and misery for the entire world ever since. For many centuries, the Turkish Ottoman Empire dominated practically the entirety of the Middle East, and enforced its own rule of law across the land. Dozens of ethnicities, languages, and religions all compose the extremely diverse peoples of the empire that had one point span from Hungary to Yemen. The Ottoman Empire had regions within it, but it had no tolerance for nations, and nationalism within the empire of any kind was always ruthlessly repressed and crushed in the name of greater imperial stability and peace. But the Ottoman Empire eventually entered into a long period of gradual decline, and by the 19th century, the rising British Empire was growing increasingly interested in the Middle East, that the Ottomans had so thoroughly dominated for so long. The Middle East, you see, geographically stood in the way between the British home islands and the crown's most important colony, India. In order to better safeguard British ships traveling between India and the home islands, the British began gradually acquiring protectorates across the Middle East, beginning with a modern United Arab Emirates in 1820, and then gradually expanding from there to include southern Yemen, Bahrain, and eventually Egypt itself in 1888. Nearly 20 years after the Suez Canal had first been completed, which granted the British the quickest possible journey time between the home islands in India by way across the Mediterranean and the Red Sea. The Suez Canal, owned by British and French shareholders despite running through Egypt, was therefore one of Britain's most geopolitically valuable assets, and they intended to ensure their continuous access to it by forcing Egypt into the status of a protectorate. The British further established protectorates over Oman and Kuwait, and then in 1914, alarmed by the increasing British meddling in the Middle East and their internal affairs. The Ottoman Empire took the decision to join the First World War on the side of Imperial Germany. Shortly after that, when the British and the French decided that the Ottoman Empire would have to be completely destroyed by them, but it was initially unclear what exactly would take the place of the centuries-old rule of the Ottomans. In 1916, British and French diplomats named Mark Sykes and Francois George Pico negotiated a secret treaty regarding how the respective governments would divide and partition the lands of the Ottoman Empire between themselves after the war's conclusion and the ancient empire's destruction. The agreement became simply known as the Sykes Pico agreement, and basically, when it follows. This zone would fall under direct colonial British rule. This zone would in time become an independent Arab state under British influence. This zone would fall under direct colonial French rule. This zone would in time become another independent Arab state but under French influence. This zone would be given to the Russian Empire while the region of Palestine and Jerusalem, with its immense religious significance, would be placed under some kind of as of yet unspecified international administration. These initial political lines that the British and French diplomats drew across the Middle East for the future of the region in 1916, paid little attention to the complex real borders of the Middle East's various religious beliefs, languages, and ethnicities. But the lines were also not just arbitrarily drawn, either. While not based on ethnicity, language, or religion, or really even geography in many cases, the lines were instead based upon the older Ottoman internal divisions that they had created, which also had little to no regard for ethnic or religious boundaries in their vast ethnically, linguistically, and religiously diverse empire, where nationalism and separatism was never tolerated. The British zone initially encompassed the Ottoman provinces of Baghdad and Basra, and parts of Derazor in Damascus, while the French zone initially encompassed the Ottoman provinces of Mosul, Derazor, Aleppo, Adana, Beirut, and Lebanon. While the international Palestine area was based on the province of Jerusalem itself, they reported directly to the Ottoman capital. This was the map that Sykes and Pico drafted their own new lines atop of in 1916, rather than any detailed ethnic, linguistic, or religious map of the region, resulting in massive consequences that reverberated down the decades ever since. The initial terms of the 1916 Sykes Pico agreement were then even further complicated by several other British wartime promises, guarantees, and assumptions. In order to encourage an Arab revolt against their Turkish overlords, many British officials guaranteed that they would favor the creation of a large, unified Arab state. Across the Arab lands following the end of the war, and nearly the same time though in 1917, Britain also proclaimed the Ball Four Declaration, which promised that the British government would favor the creation of a national home for the Jewish people in the land of Palestine, and then finally, there was the question of oil. Oil had been first discovered in the Middle East, and a remote part of Iran in 1908, that the British subsequently dominated through their state-owned Anglo-Persian oil company, which were later evolved into the British petroleum or BP company that we know of today. But Iran's oil at the time wasn't considered enough. World War I was proving to everyone involved how critical oil was becoming as a strategic resource to fuel the new machines of war. From battleships to airplanes, and the United States was at that time by far the world's largest producer and supplier of oil. America therefore provided the British Empire with most of their oil needs, but America's president at the time was also Woodrow Wilson, a man who was staunchly opposed to the concept of empires in the post-war world, including Britons. So believing America to be a potentially unreliable provider of oil to their empire in the future, the British government was, at the time, highly incentivized to acquire influence and control over as much of the Middle East nearby to Iran as possible, where they believed that the potential future massive oil deposits would be later discovered and owned by them, providing Britain with the critical oil it would need to fight through whatever conflict would come to them next. After World War I concluded, the victorious allies met at Versailles in 1919 to set the terms of peace, and the borders of the Middle East were shifted once again. The Ottoman province of Mosul was transferred from the French zone of influence over to the British zone of influence, and as the Ottoman Empire was actively collapsing, the allies pressed the infamous terms of the Treaty of Severes upon the dying empire in 1920, the terms of which made even the Treaty of Versailles that was pressed upon Germany seemed fairly generous. The terms of the previous seismicoe agreement between Britain and France would remain intact. With the Russian Empire now gone, a huge independent state of Armenia was to be carved out of Northeastern Anatolia. Another independent state for the Kurdish people was to be further carved out of Southeastern Anatolia. Secondly, it was to seize control over several of the empires of Gianislands. Greece was to annex much of Eastern Thraces and a chunk around the city of Izmir on the Anatolian mainland, while the area around the strategically important Turkish straits was to fall under an international regime outside of Turkey's remaining jurisdiction. The Turkish state that was to be left over was doomed to become nothing more than an insignificant rump state, and thus outraged by the Treaty's terms. The emerging Turkish government almost immediately rebelled and continued fighting against the allies for the next two years. After the Turkish forces made significant military gains in the battlefield, they forced the allies to return back to the negotiating table again, and concluded with the terms of the 1923 Treaty of Lossan, which replaced the terms of the previous Treaty of Sivrez, and were far more favorable to Turkey. Finally, the borders of the modern Middle East, as we roughly know them as today, were set in ink. But the terms of Lossan in 1923 still left many questions and problems unresolved. There would be no independent state created for the Kurdish people after all, for example, a problem that has vexed the region for the century ever since. Today, the Kurds are one of the largest stateless people in the world, numbering some 40 million strong with their own separate ethnic, cultural, and linguistic identity, but divided between the borders of what became Turkey, Syria, Iraq, and Iran, where in all four countries they are minority groups and have often been subject to persecution and discrimination. British nationalism and separatism and the desire to create an independent Kurdish state out of these areas has been a constant flashpoint of conflict in this region ever since. The British also smushed together the former Ottoman provinces of Basul, Baghdad, and Basra into their mandate for Mesopotamia, which later evolved into the state of Iraq. But the problem with that was that all three of those Ottoman provinces contained very different people with very different interests, identities, and beliefs. The people of Mesul were mostly ethnically Kurdish. The people of the Baghdad province were primarily Sunni Muslim Arabs, while the people of the Basra province were mostly Shia Muslim Arabs. Even further, what became Kuwait had also been a formal part of the Basra province under the Ottoman administration.

transcript: This is Beaverton, Oregon. It's within this wooded, comparatively quiet Portland suburb where Nike's been headquartered since the 70s. While 67% of the company's total revenue comes from shoes, nowhere on this campus will you actually find a shoe factory. No where in the US nor the world, in fact, will you find a Nike-owned shoe factory because Nike's not a shoemaker. Its business is more abstract. The company's SEC filings will tell you it's not a shoe company at all, but rather list the Nike and swish design trademarks as its most valuable assets. To fill night, the company's founder now in his late 80s, Nike's business is inspiring and innovating for athletes while the filings call it a company that provides design, development, and worldwide marketing and selling of athletic footwear, apparel, equipment, accessory, and services. In other words, Nike's not a shoemaker, but a tastemaker. And tastemaking when it comes to shoes is a pretty fickle business. Shoes are tools, but not just for walking and working. A study by researchers at the University of Kansas has shown that when subjects are just shown someone's footwear without any other context, they're able to guess with fairly high accuracy the age, gender, income range, and even personality traits and political leanings of the person in question. The finding only backs what consumers already knew though. People judge based on shoes and recognize they get judged based on shoes, so they make shoe choices to cultivate an image or tell their own personal story, whether that be who they are or who they aspire to be. Extroverts will reach for norm disrupting red shoes while those hoping to not sick out will prioritize quieter shoes and bland styles, for instance. One way or another, we use shoes to understand others, and so we in turn select shoes to help frame how people think of ourselves. Shoes and shoe selection tell stories, and Nike used to be the best storyteller. If you don't know the story of Nike's rise, it's laid out across the map of the company's campus. At the end of one bowerman drive, named after the company co-founder, on the shore of Lake Nike, neighboring the Michael Jordan and John McElroy buildings, and across from the Ronaldo field, is Steve Prefontaine Hall, appropriately at the core of the campus. Few characters embody the Nike story like Steve Prefontaine or Pre, and that's by design. Before Nike was a giant, before it was even called Nike, it was little more than the young, ambitious Phil Knight, a lifetime runner himself, importing sleek Japanese running shoes. It was a disruptor, a plucky underdog, a fighter looking to carve out just a bit of market share in a nascent American jogging boom at the beginning of the 1970s. At about the same time that Nike started gaining momentum, a scrappy runner from an old coastal logging town in Oregon was beginning to rise. As Pre began to establish himself as America's best distance runner, it was only natural that he joined the brand. But Pre's central to Nike's rise, not just because he was good at track, but because of how he went about being good at Prefontaine won a host of collegiate titles, he set American records, he went to Munich in 1972 to represent the US at the Olympics, he was an undeniable talent. He was also a character, he was a working class kid from a tough industrial town, he was small, scrappy, handsome and brash, he was cocky but he backed it up with a work ethic that's still revered in American running culture. He appeared on the cover of Sports Illustrated at 19, his races were aired on National TV, his go pre t-shirts became so popular they were met by a backlash of stop pre-shirts, he was an American star and he wore Nike. When signed to the company, pre-rept Nike shoes at run clubs and public meetups, he traveled to high school and college programs to demo them, he sent them to friends and rivals around the world, he pushed the product and the producer, a brash upstart of its own, embodied the kid's spirit. Nike was doing it their own way too, with shoes that pushed the boundaries first with the Nike Cortez that added midsole cushioning for runners. They then took the technology another step forward with the Nike waffle trainer, designed to be lightweight and grippy while staying comfortable and padded. Ultimately, a fatal car crash claimed pre before he was able to wear his own signature shoe, the pre Montreal's, but his indomitable spirit had catalyzed a movement. Title IX and televised track races and marathons helped spur a running boom that saw an estimated 25 million new runners enter the scene in the US across the decade and they wanted to be like pre. They went with Nike, the upstart all about running and innovating comfortable shoes that were also worn by the most iconic and inspirational runner the US had produced. The running revolution transformed the American shoe landscape too. As running took off and the shoes became more comfortable, it became okay to wear trainers not to train but never-day life. At the beginning of the 1970s, Athletic shoes only accounted for one in every 12 pairs sold. By 1979, it was one in four pairs. The shoe game had transformed and Nike was in the middle of it. By 1980, Nike made up 50% of all running shoe sales in the US and was quickly closing the gap on a deedus in total athletic shoe sales. And with the success of the pre partnership, Nike now had a model to grow into other spaces too. It recognized a trend, in this case an uptick in running and jogging in the US, it found someone who embodied the movement and then it leaned into it, creating further demand and momentum within the movement. It recognized what was becoming cool and helped make it cool at the same time. But fashion changes fast, and in the 80s, culture went urban. Jogging shoes weren't cool, basketball shoes were. It caught Nike flat footed, reporting its first losses in 1984 and sending it scrambling to reinstate Phil Knight's active control of the company to fight back on the encroaching converse, new balance, and Reebok. Nike had the model, but neither the shoe nor the athlete. Then it set its crosshairs on a young promising player in Michael Jordan. While the legacy may speak for itself today, as Michael Jordan would become the best basketball player in the world and the Jordan brand, a branch of Nike, when it itself become one of the largest shoe brands in the world, this strategy at the time was extremely bold. Not the first pick in the draft and playing a position not yet associated with basketball dominance, Jordan was hardly locked to be the face of the league when he went into the National Basketball Association. Nike was making a bet on upside. Without a past in basketball shoes and far behind the converse and Adidas basketball brands, they had to be bold to compete and creative to find a niche to gain a foot hold in the space and to convince an entirely unconvinced Jordan to even join the brand in the first place. They melded bold and creative with a shoe design and marketing campaign all in one. At the time, basketball shoes to be worn in the NBA had to be 51% the color white, but Nike had an idea. Make a flashy, colorful Michael Jordan shoe, ignore the rule, and simply deal with the consequences. It'd be worth a visibility regardless, it'd fit the brand, and it'd excite the player. In 1984, a rookie Michael Jordan took the court with shoes that intentionally broke the rules and Nike paid the $5,000 fine. It was genius. Ultimately, he'd only wear them on three occasions that season as the NBA promised to make a stronger stand on the issue should have persist, but three times proved plenty. They pushed the Jordan One on the message that not only is this your new favorite player shoe, it's a shoe so radical and counterculture it's banned from the game it's designed for. The shoe, in turn, sold out immediately and totaled $126 million in sales in its first year. Nike stuck to the same playbook as they grew into other realms. It was pre-fantane and running in the 70s, it was Jordan and basketball in the 80s, it was Tiger Woods and Golf in the 90s, Serena Williams and Tennis alongside Ronaldo and Soccer in the 2000s. Nike became the world's biggest by backing the moldbreakers and acting like they were the bold up-and-comers themselves. They had an edge, they were for winners, they didn't make excuses, they just did it and customers took it hook, line, and sinker. But a problem and a fundamental one at that. When a company is born out of the idea of a scrappy underdog disruptor but succeeds to the point where it's now the industry leader, it's hard to maintain that same identity and relentless drive. Fast forward to the 2026 Winter Olympic Games. What you see is Team USA covered head to toe in Nike. But this isn't a picture of Nike's maintained dominance, rather it's what the company hopes will be the beginning of a comeback story. These American athletes are wearing a Nike sublabel called All Conditions Gear or ACG and the fact that this hasn't reached name brand recognition across a public audience highlights one of the brand's many fumbles in the past 20 years. The running shoe market has taken a unique path in the last decade. Running shoes of all sorts are as popular as ever, but trail running shoes, a comparatively rugged off-road option, are especially hot. While trail runners make up a fraction of total running shoe sales, a number cited at 12% in 2022, they're steadily outpacing conventional running shoes and projected annual compound growth. Running shoes are in, but trail runners are even more in. And just as trainers, basketball shoes and running shoes found their way into casual fashion in the past, now so too, have trail runners. Brands specializing in maximally cushioned trail shoes like Hoka have warmed their way into everyday shoe rotations for service jobs and weekend chores alike. Solomon's rugged mountain running shoes have unexpectedly become popular with Gen Zers in the urban environs of New York City. It goes further, too.

name: Atlas Pro
video_title: Why Do Redwoods ONLY Grow In California?
transcript: Recently, I traveled to California in search of an extinct butterfly. It's a bit of a long story, but one of the environments I had the pleasure to explore on this trip was the coastal redwood forest native only to the seaside mountains of this state. Gazing up in awe at these tremendous trees and seeing how successful they are here, I couldn't help but wonder why they remain limited to just this one area on the planet. I mean, despite its absolute dominance where it grows, the natural range of the coastal redwood remains restricted to this one seemingly random narrow chain of mountains here on the Pacific coast of the Americas. The story is similar for their only close relative, the Giant Redwood, which also only inhabits small pockets of the nearby Sierra Nevada mountains. Yet the great size of these trees is testimony enough to their ability to outcompete just about anything. So why haven't they outcompeted everything? Why do redwoods only grow in California? Okay, so as usual, the only way to really understand these trees is to start at the very beginning of their evolutionary history, which once again brings us back 200 million years to a time when Pangaea was fully intact. It was here that the very first cypress trees evolved, arguably the earliest ancestors of redwoods. Finding themselves on such an interconnected landmass, these primitive cypress trees managed to spread across the entirety of the supercontinent. Fast forward 100 million years and by this time Pangaea had started to break apart, resulting in two major continents, Gondwana in the south and Larasia in the north. And naturally, this fractured the cypress family into two distinct populations as well. Those on Gondwana didn't really fare so well as it was mostly a sweltering hot desert by this time. However, being the landmass further from the equator on average, Larasia experienced relatively cooler weather conditions compared to Gondwana. This caused a persistent fog to hover over much of the continent, delivering a slow but constant supply of water to the Larasian forests. And it was under these conditions that a member of the cypress family adapted to take advantage of this airborne water source, branching off into a new lineage, Sequoia Day. Their strategy was simple, sacrifice much of their root development in exchange for a greater ability to intake water directly from the air. This might not sound like a good tradeoff in today's colder, drier earth, but millions of years ago, the hot and humid conditions caused soils to lose moisture much faster while it hung around in the air for longer, rewarding this new tactic of water intake. This lended in advantage to the Sequoia family that allowed them to spread far across this landmass, coming to inhabit Asia, Europe, and yes, North America. We know this because Sequoia Day fossils have been found across every remnant of this ancient supercontinent, suggesting that redwood forests were once widespread across the northern lands. And it was the great success experienced by the Sequoias during this time that opened the door for certain members of this family to continue specializing even further by growing taller and taller. name: Now You See It
video_title: Succession - How To Film a Car Scene
transcript: When shooting a scene, there's nothing more constraining than two people in the back of a car. It's small, it's cramped, and everybody faces the same direction. But let's take a look at how, despite these limitations, the TV show's succession managed to use creative cinematic techniques in three scenes, where two characters, Shiv Roy and Tom Wombsgans, do nothing but talk in the back of a car. In the first car scene in Season 1 Episode 9, Tom and Shiv drive to their wedding venue in rural England. The scene begins with a plain medium shot, and the lighting, custom design, and color grading all reminds the viewer that we're no longer in the busy and colorful New York City, but in the dreary and overcast UK. Like the shot, the dialogue starts off innocuous, but then Shiv goes into what she's really thinking about, and the camera works shifts. Um, hey, I wanted to ask you something. Uh-oh. From here, we go to a close-up on Shiv as she asks Tom for details on the crew's situation. I could do with knowing about the bad thing in cruises. Tom dances around the issue and Shiv continues to push, and the camera work reflects the newfound tension. Each time Shiv asks Tom a question, the camera starts on her, and then pans over to his response. This camera move happens not once, not twice, but three times in a row, and it gives Shiv all the power, while also forcing us to watch Tom squirm. But I did then destroy all the evidence, so... But then finally, Tom pushes back, and it becomes a tennis match, with the camera bouncing from Shiv to Tom and back to Shiv again. It's not even that bad. Oh, it's quite bad. It's quite bad. From here, we don't return to the medium shot, and instead leave the car entirely, showing the conflict never resolved. Even when stuck in a car, the camera tells the story here. It shows innocent beginnings and interrogation, and then a standoff with no resolution. And succession isn't just skilled at dramatic scenes. They also know how to make the back of a car funny. In our second scene, in Season 2, Episode 4, Tom and Shiv head to the waystar Royco headquarters in New York City. Right from the first shot, the scene feels completely different. We return to that warm, busy New York City Hue. The initial medium shot is handheld instead of fixed, and it's more casual. We've dropped the seat belts and the tense dialogue. This initial shot sets the scene up perfectly for comedy, especially with the wide-angle lens stretching Tom and Shiv's features, making every hand gesture an eye movement bigger. And when we go to close-ups, the camera moves in between them and closer to their faces, further exaggerating their movements. And all those differences, when combined, completely alter the feeling of these scenes, one traumatic and one comedic. You know, church and state, and ACDC, and ebony and ivory, and never the twanchial meat. It's like you and your dad have finally admitted how much you're into each other. Now you can, you know, not this, but now you can bang. Still in a car, still just talking, but a totally different feeling. And that brings us to perhaps the most famous shot in the entire show, which is, you guessed it, Tom and Shiv sitting in the back of a car. I won't spoil the ending of the show, but this third car scene takes place at the end of the series finale, as Tom and Shiv drive out of waystar Royco forever changed. After Tom enters the car, the scene famously holds on a single medium shot. The frame is almost completely black. Visually, they could be driving to a funeral. Despite holding on just a single, static shot, the moment is surprisingly dynamic. As we swirl through the underbelly of the waystar Royco building, we get the famous hand touch in the middle, and then we're forced to do nothing but look between Tom and Shiv. Notice how the lights in the basement alternate between lighting up Tom, then Shiv, then Tom again. And notice the distance between the two characters in the frame, compared to the other two scenes. These lighting and framing differences show the profound distance now between Tom and Shiv, lit darkly and separately, framed far apart, but still somehow touching in the middle. Without dialogue and without a camera move, we get a story of their tragic union. Succession is a show of mostly people sitting around talking, and yet the creator still managed to make it tense, funny, and heartbreaking. Now you see it.

name: The Art Assignment
video_title: What Have I Been Doing?
transcript: You haven't heard from me in a really long time. Actually, the last time I was in front of this bookcase was in August 2020. You know what? This feels really weird. Let's go somewhere else. Ah, okay. This is better. Anyway, when last we spoke, I told you I was going to be taking a hiatus for a while, but then would come back and post every so often. I didn't think it would take me two years, but you know how things go. What have I been doing instead? Great question. I would love to tell you. I've just been watching TikTok like the rest of you. Just kidding. The first thing I've been doing is making a lot of art. After seven plus years of talking about art and telling you guys to do assignments, I'm taking my own advice and making things myself. Part of this was a pandemic necessity. Seriously, for a good stretch of 2020, making art was my only loose tether to reality and calm. But I also just remember that I really like to make things. Creating collages for friends and for fundraisers like the project for awesome was a way for me to get out of my head, combing through vintage magazines, and playing around with images and words until something felt right, or even just sort of right. This is a series of collages I made using some old calendars I had of images by architectural photographer Julia Schulman, mixed with images I cut out of magazines. Are they going to win any awards? Surely not, but that's okay. They bring me great satisfaction. While I'm making them, I think about all the things that are different today than they were when the pictures were taken. I also get to give something to someone else and communicate on this other level, which just makes me happy. I also took an online workshop earlier this year with the artist Elizabeth Hadel about illustrating personal power symbols. We did this exercise called inner self-portrait, where you write a list of words that describe what's going on in your head at the moment. And then you make a pie chart of them that forms a head and then add a neck and shoulders and decorate as you like. We also made what Hadel calls sigils, mysterious or magical symbols that describe personal goals or points of focus. I wanted to focus on the word open, so I played around with the form and came up with this one that felt right. And then I tried making it in these different color variations, which was really fun. I also started taking pottery, which I really, really enjoy. Here you're seeing a bit of my process, which probably doesn't look very impressive, but is way better than where I started out. Pottery is one of those 10,000 hours things that you just have to do a lot to be any good at. And even then, you still mess up a lot. Failure is part of pottery, and there's something really satisfying about that when you can embrace it. There are also so many steps and stages and tricks and tools and methods. The things you think are going to be great come out of the kiln looking like poo. And then the ones you curse about in almost scrap turn out to be your most favorite things. You can also fix things you mess up and turn a pot with a gloopy, heavy bottom into something that actually looks like a bowl. There's also the absolute joy of getting things back from the kiln. These humble lumps of clay have been transformed into objects that you can actually drink coffee out of, or just stick somewhere and admire. The other thing I've been doing is looking at art. Sure I'd been doing that before, but it's really nice to look at art without thinking about making content about it. Not thinking about whether it would represent well on camera, or if it would lose its magic when seen through a lens. Here we're breezing through the many photos I've taken of art in the last two years. Admittedly not many in 2020, but picking up steam from there. I do take photos to post online sometimes, but often it's just for reference, making sure to snap pictures of labels too. Sometimes you don't know what's going to stick in your head for the long term. And I find myself circling back to my art picks often, to remember a name, make a connection between something else I've seen, or give me ideas for my own art. Before I take any pictures, I almost always spend time with the art first. Just looking and walking around, and trying to listen to my thoughts and any questions or responses that might bubble up. I realize during the pandemic how much I miss being in the presence of art and the ideas of others in physical form. It makes such a difference to be there. And when I could start to leave the house and see art again, it felt both like being with old friends I hadn't seen in years, and also like meeting a bunch of new friends and being super grateful for the opportunity. While I've been not making YouTube videos, I've been thinking about the difficulty of capturing art experiences in any single format. Horizontal footage is just better for documenting certain things. And long-form video is important for communicating more nuanced histories and ideas. But vertical video is definitely better for other kinds of art experiences. And short-form tends to be more playful, more fun, and more likely to keep someone's attention. It can be less polished and more authentic feeling, too. I've started to experiment with shorts here on YouTube and also on TikTok and Reels, where I'll continue to post as I travel and see things and make things and feel like sharing them. I've also been working on a longer-form project with the brilliant minds over at Bright Trip that I'm really excited to share with you. It's an online course on how to appreciate art, which we filmed in and around Washington, D.C. in some outstanding locations. In the course, I share a ton of my secrets for how to relax and enjoy yourself in the company of art, and also how to talk about it and share your experience with others. I give tips on photographing art, and also what you can do to prepare for art viewing in advance. It's not the usual art appreciation course about composition or schools or movements. Those things are fine, sure, but to me, minimally helpful when art can be so many different kinds of things. With this course, I really wanted to share with you my own relationship with art, and break down very practically how I interact with art and incorporate it into my everyday life. The course is now live, and I hope you'll consider giving it to yourself or someone else that you like. You can find a link to the course in the video info and comments below. Over the last two years, I've been thinking a lot about how we integrate internet life and real life, and with the previous seven years being a situation where I was primarily living online, I've been attempting to better know my own physical geographic community. One of the ways I've been doing that is stepping back into real life curating. Instead of the more nebulous form, I've been practicing through the art assignment. I was part of the curatorial team for this year's butter, a black fine art fair that takes place annually in Indianapolis. These are images of the final installation of the show, but I loved everything leading up to this finished moment, doing studio visits with artists, hashing out placement of artworks with colleagues I admire, seeing how different artworks relate to each other, and actually talking with other people about art in the presence of art. It was magical, and I'm looking forward to doing more stuff like this in the future. There is of course a lot of other stuff I've been doing during the past two years, including trying to raise two human children, being a decent wife, maintaining my health and friendships, and also working on a lot of other super secret future projects. But really, I just wanted to say hi, and let you know that I haven't disappeared. And if you want to keep up with me, there are still several ways to do that. I hope you haven't disappeared either, and I'd love to hear what you've been up to. Let me know in the comments, okay? I hope you enjoyed this video. Thanks for watching.

name: Lessons from the Screenplay
video_title: The Anatomy of Genres — A Conversation with John Truby | @BeyondTheScreenplay
transcript: Hey guys, long time no see. I'm sharing this video on the Lesson from the Screenplay channel because I think it's a great new resource for all you writers out there. John Truby, whose book The Anatomy of Story I have talked about many times on this channel, has a new book out called The Anatomy of genres, and it's great. We recently had John Truby on our podcast to discuss the book, chat about some movies, and break down why people don't talk enough about genre if they talk about it at all. We had a really fascinating conversation, and I think you're going to find it very useful. Now, I know many of you have been asking when a new video essay is going to be released, and my honest answer is I don't know. As I mentioned on Twitter earlier this year, I'm currently working with Bioware on the next installment of Mass Effect. It's a dream come true gig, and all of my time and energy is currently focused on that, as well as a few other side projects. For now, I hope you really enjoy this special episode of Beyond the Screenplay, a conversation with John Truby. Hi, I'm Michael, and welcome to Beyond the Screenplay. The podcast where each episode we do a conversational deep-dive analysis into a film. Today, we're doing something different, so I'm here with part of the Beyond the Screenplay team, Trisha Rand. Hello, everyone. And we are joined by a very special guest, so anyone who is familiar with the Lesson from the Screenplay YouTube channel has surely heard me say probably more times than they care, in his book The Anatomy of Story, John Truby writes, because it's one of, if not the most referenced book in the videos on Lesson from the Screenplay. He has a new book out called The Anatomy of genres, how story forms explain the way the world works, and he is here to chat with us about it. John Truby, welcome to the show. Well, thank you for having me. I couldn't be more excited to be here with you in this fantastic podcast and talking about my favorite subject. Yes, so I'm also really excited, so just as a little bit of history, so your previous book The Anatomy of Story was kind of the first screenplay book that clicked for me, and it's not overstating things to say that I don't think Lesson from the Screenplay would be what it became without that book, because it was the first time, you know, I think you have this really great ability to take things that I feel like implicitly, I kind of had a fuzzy idea of, because I've seen so many movies, and I know, you know, there's something at work here, but I can't quite put my finger on it, and you're able to sharpen those ideas and focus them so that then you can like grab a hold of them and use them as a tool. And so I really, really loved Anatomy of Story. It opened my eyes to so many things, and Anatomy of Genre is awesome, and going to do the same thing, and you're getting in deeper more detail, and I'm really excited about all of it. So kind of just a start, I'm curious like why, what made you want to write the Anatomy of Genre, and how do you feel like it's a continuation or a partner, or what's its relationship to the Anatomy of Story? Well, it's actually a very interesting evolution on how it came about. It was the product of about 50 years of work, and the last five years of writing. So it's been quite the project. I wasn't quite sure I could finish a marathon that went on for that long, but luckily I did. But to give me some background about how this book came about, you know, a lot of times when I talk to writers about what I do, they say, oh, yeah, I know all about Story. They say, I use 3x structure, or I use the hero's journey, or I use Save the Cat. And they think, well, that's all I need. And here's the problem, and I really don't like having to tell them this. But these books are fine for beginners, but they have very few practical story techniques. And certainly, almost nothing that can tell you how to write at the professional level. And I always have to to tell writers, remember, when I was just talking about, wouldn't it be nice to write a script? No, we're talking about being in the top 1% of writers, selling your material, and ideally creating your own brand. And so when I wrote the Anatomy of Story, my goal was to include all the professional story techniques a writer would need in order to write a best-selling novel or a screenplay that actually sells. But the one subject it doesn't cover, which is now the key to writing a hit film or novel, is how to write the different genres that make up 99% of popular storytelling today. And what I believe is that writers who want to succeed professionally have to write the stories that the business, and by that I mean the studios, the publishers, and of course the readers, want to buy. And that means that the storytelling game is won by mastering the structure of genres. And that means, first of all, mastering the 15 to 20 story beats that you need to each form. So that was the original motivation for writing the Anatomy of genres. And in fact, the first half of each chapter goes through these plot beats, the 15 to 20 unique plot beats, in great detail to show you how to write them as well as the order that they are most dramatic in terms of expressing whatever this story problem is to the reader and the audience. And these are the beats that have to be in this story. And then as I'm sure get into, the second half of each chapter is a whole lot more. It's a whole lot bigger. And it's an area, it's an area of theme of that is probably the most misunderstood aspect of all writing. Yeah. Yeah. I'm so glad that you bring up theme because I was loving listeners of our show, know that I'm obsessed with theme. But I was just loving that the genres, you know, I have long advocated for people to understand genre as critical in understanding theme because certain kinds of themes are innately explored in certain genres or to put it in another way. Certain themes are embedded in the very nature of certain genres. And, you know, in my sort of, I guess, haphazard way, I've always had a vague idea of what those are. Westerns are about civilization. I kind of understand this and justice and these kinds of things. And obviously, you know, crime is dealing with morality and justice. And these kinds of vague notions in my mind about, well, this theme makes sense because the genre inherently deals with it. But I love that the book is organized completely organized according to what themes each genre sort of addresses. So can you speak a little bit to that? I'm so glad you picked up on that because that to me was the most exciting part of writing this book and the most revelatory part for me of writing this book. In the opening chapter, I talk about the three unwritten rules by which the entire entertainment business is based and that means film, novels, television. And the first of them is, it's a genre world. You have to master the particular genre you're writing. And you have to know it and execute it better than anybody else. The second major rule is that you succeed by mixing genres. In others, we don't have in any of these mediums single genre stories anymore. It's typically a combination of two, three, even four forms, which allows you to express a great deal of plot, much more plot than a single genre story allows you to do. But the real trick, the real key to setting yourself apart and really nailing the genre that you work in is the third rule, which is that in order to be successful and set yourself apart from everyone else writing that particular genre story, you have to transcend the form. And that means, first of all, you have to twist the beats. There's three ways you do it. You twist the beats or you execute the beats in a way that we've never seen before. But the second one is that each genre expresses a deeper life philosophy through this, through the thing. And if you're going to be successful, you have to know what that life philosophy is. And you have to then express it through your story. You know, immediately writers run into problem with theme because they've heard the old Samuel Goldwyn line of, you know, if you want to send a message, send it, Western Union, right? Yeah. They don't want to be on the nose. They don't want to hit you over their head with it and preach to the audience. And they're absolutely right about that. They don't want to do that. But what they do is they go to the other extreme. They completely devoid descript of theme. That's the biggest mistake you can make because what these, what these viewers are coming back to the same genre every time for is not because of the plot beats. They know the plot beats. They've seen it thousands of times. Yes, they want to see it done in a different way. They want you to surprise their expectations. But what they're really coming back for is they love the theme.

name: TED-Ed
video_title: How do snakes swallow animals so much bigger than they are? - Niko Zlotnik
transcript: How can a bigger tube fit inside a smaller tube? This might sound like a riddle, but it's a practical dilemma in our snake-eat snake world. This eastern king snake, for example, has a mouthful of a Texas rat snake, but the rat snakes longer than it is. So how can the king snake possibly swallow it whole? This is just one of countless predatory dilemmas snakes have solved. Since slithering onto the scene, some 150 million years ago, evolving length and limblessness out of their ancestral lizard forms, snakes have diversified rapidly. The nudely bodies and flexible heads granted them access to novel places and prey. And today, there are nearly 4,000 snake species spanning habitats high and low, wet and dry. All are carnivorous, but their diets range from fish eggs to alligators. Before dropping a matinee, allows most snakes to swallow their mules, holes. Because their jaw bones aren't fused like ours, but connected by an elastic ligament, and the bones on the sides of their lower jaws flare apart, they can dramatically stretch their mores. Prediculated pythons can even achieve 180 degree gapes. Sharp, curved teeth, also line many snake's jaws, keeping prey from wriggling out. And to prevent suffocation when choking down a big meal, many snakes shift the position of their airways entrance and isolate which ribcage regions they inhale with. These are also equipped with stretchy tissue along their jaws, enabling them to spread four times wider than their own skulls. They've been documented using this skill to eat hyenas, alligators, and yes, evil humans. Whole. Their skin might sag after all the stretching, and it could take weeks to metabolize meals this large. But it seems their intestines have special cells for helping digest bones, and they can sometimes survive for over a year on just one feast. African egg-eating snakes, meanwhile, consume large intact bird eggs, pissing their shells in their esophageye, using inward-facing, vertebral spines. While many snakes make a show of swallowing things whole, gravity snakes often pry their prey's limbs off one by one, and blind snakes decapitate their termite targets, probably to prioritize their more digestible bodies. Some blind snakes actually use chemical secretions to repel their prey, because it allows them to hang out in ant colonies, snacking on their inhabitants without being attacked. Their skills have even been noticed by other species. Eastern screech owls sometimes place blind snakes in their nest, where they eat the insects that might otherwise harm their young. In fact, baby owls with a blind snake nest mate have higher rates of growth and survival. The same can't be said for other species. Turtle-headed sea snakes scrape fish eggs off coral reefs, and cat-eyed snakes hunt red-eyed tree frog eggs. However, even as embryos, the developing frogs consents an attacking snake's vibrations and may prematurely hatch to escape. It's a sacrifice, but at least it's not certain death by serpent. Gata snakes, meanwhile, go after Western Newts, even though their skin is packed with a potent neurotoxin. Just one bite could kill a person. The Gata snakes swallow the newts unscathed, since modified proteins in their nerve cells prevent the toxin from binding. Not only that, but since the toxin may remain in their livers for weeks afterwards, it may even end up lending Gata snakes' protection against their predators. However, snakes more often make their own toxins, injecting or spitting venom from specialized glands out of grooved or syringe-like fangs. Philippine cobre venom is full of fast-acting neurotoxins that cause paralysis and respiratory fave. West African, sore-scaled viper venom is a cocktail of compounds that cause profuse bleeding and tissue death. And while inland tie-pans are thought to possess the world's most potent venom, they tend to reserve it for quickly killing rodents. But we still haven't answered. How smaller snakes can consume bigger ones. It turns out that the king's snake does this with an elegant trick. Once it runs out of space in its flexible stomach, it stretches and compresses its spine, shoving the rat's snake into a kinked zigzag shape within its digestive tract. So a post-meal x-ray may look like this. Sorry. We know the truth can be hard to swallow unless, of course, you're a snake. Ready to keep learning? Watch these videos to either challenge what you think you know or learn more about what you don't. Subscribe and stay curious.

name: Science Etonnante
video_title: L'intelligence artificielle dans les jeux vidéo
transcript: J'ai travaillé pendant sept ans dans l'industrie du jeu vidéo et une chose qui m'a surpris au début, c'est l'usage qu'on y faisait du terme IA. D'habitude, quand on parle d'intelligence artificielle, on pense réseau de neurones, diplônes, nignes ou modèles de langage. Mais non, quand on parle de l'IA d'un jeu, ça désigne généralement bien autre chose. Ce qu'on appelle intelligence artificielle dans le jeu vidéo, c'est l'ensemble des systèmes qui gèrent les comportements, des ennemis, des alliés, des passants, tout ce qu'on regroupe parfois sous le terme de personnage non joueur ou PNG. C'est-à-dire toutes les entités autonomes qui peuple les univers virtuels dans lesquels on joue. Et pour donner vie à tous ces PNG, les développeurs n'utilisent généralement pas des réseaux de neurones, mais ils s'appuient sur des techniques qui sont en fait assez différentes de celles de l'intelligence artificielle le moderne. Et c'est de cela dont je vais vous parler aujourd'hui. Alors petit autopromo, si je vous parle de ça, c'est notamment parce que je sors un livre consacré à la science des jeux vidéo. Ça s'appelle le Labo du jeu vidéo, et j'y parle de graphisme, de physique, d'hier, de probabilité, d'économie ou encore de psychologie. Et j'explique comment toutes ces disciplines scientifiques interviennent à différents moments de la création d'un jeu vidéo. Je vous en dis plus à la fin d'épisode. Pour comprendre comment fonctionne l'IA des jeux vidéo, il n'est pas une utile de commencer par un bref panorama historique de ce qu'on appelle l'intelligence artificielle. Tout d'abord, l'IA, c'est une discipline presque aussi ancienne que l'informatique. Et son objectif en général, c'est de faire réaliser par une machine des tâches cognitives généralement réalisées par des humains. Dès les années 1950, à partir du moment où la programmation permettait d'utiliser des fonctions conditionnelles du genre IF ceci zen fait cela, on a pu concevoir des programmes informatiques capables de simuler de façon sommaire des comportements de décision, tel que les humains pourraient les prendre. Il suffisait de cartographier comment les humains s'y prenaient et de répliquer ça dans le programme. Ça a été la naissance de ce qu'on a appelé l'IA symbolique. Alors symbolique, ça veut dire, en gros, que tout ce qu'on veut que la machine effectue, on l'a explicitement programmé, ou du moins on arrive à le relier à des consignes précises qu'on aura volontairement implementé généralement avec l'aide d'un expert du domaine concerné. Un exemple fameux d'IA symbolique, c'est le chatbot Elisa, conçu en 1966, donc plus de 50 ans avant de chat GPT, et qui pouvait faire illusion pendant quelques tours de conversation. Il analysait les entrées de l'humain, reformulé des questions bateaux et tout ça en suivant des règles précises. En parallèle des approches symboliques des chercheurs ont commencé à développer ce qu'on a appelé le machine learning, l'apprentissage automatique. L'idée ici, c'est de faire en sorte qu'un programme informatique arrive à effectuer une tâche cognitif, non pas parce qu'on lui a radis explicitement comment s'y prendre, mais parce qu'on lui aura montré beaucoup d'exemples. C'est comme ça que fonctionne, par exemple, les réseaux de neurones qui sont si populaires aujourd'hui. Alors, n'autons tout de même qu'ils ont connu l'histoire mouvementée, puisque après leur invention dans les années 70 et 70, ils ont été peu à peu quasiment abandonnés. Et puis ils ont fait un comeback fraq à 100 à partir de 2012, via ce qu'on appelle maintenant le deep learning avec les réseaux de neurones profonds. Alors si les réseaux de neurones se sont soudainement mis à fonctionner, c'est pour plusieurs raisons. Bon, les progrès algorithmiques, bien sûr, la disponibilité de grande quantité donnée pour les entraînés, merci Internet, mais il y a eu aussi un facteur déterminant l'utilisation des cartes graphiques. Des processeurs qui avaient été initialement conçues pour optimiser les graphismes des jeux vidéo, et qui se sont révélés très adaptés aux opérations mathématiques demandées par l'entraînement des réseaux de neurones profonds, par exemple les multiplications de matrices. D'une certaine façon, sans les jeux vidéo, l'IA qu'on connaît aujourd'hui n'aurait jamais pu exister. Alors pour résumer, il faut bien avoir en tête cette distinction entre le machine learning qui représente l'essentiel de l'IA moderne et les approches symboliques qui ont initialement dominées, mais aujourd'hui beaucoup moins de gens utilisent. Parfois dans le milieu, l'IA symbolique, on appelle ça Good Old Fashion AI, la bonne vieille IA traditionnelle. Mais pour les PNG des jeux vidéo, et c'est ce qui m'a surpris donc, quand j'ai commencé à travailler dans le domaine il y a 8 ans, c'est quasiment toujours de la bonne vieille IA traditionnelle qu'on utilise. Le jeu vidéo est un des domaines qui reposent encore majoritairement sur de l'IA symbolique et on va voir qu'il y a des très bonnes raisons pour ça. La forme la plus classique d'IA dans le jeu vidéo, c'est pour gérer les adversaires. Si c'est un jeu solo, on a généralement besoin d'opposition et il faut donc faire se comporter le ou les ennemis de façon au moins un peu intelligente. La méthode la plus simple sur le papier, c'est d'utiliser un arbre de décision. Vous écrivez une série de conditions pour distinguer dans quelle situation le PNG se trouve, et ces conditions vont être vérifiées de façon successive pour déterminer quel comportement l'ennemi va adopter. En fonction de sa position, de son état, de ce qu'il sait de vous, les circonstances, etc. C'est super simple à implementer, c'est juste des séries de IF-DEN et on a une maîtrise totale de ce qui va se produire. Cette technique elle peut marcher par exemple pour un jeu autour partout. L'ennemi doit décider quel déplacement ou quel attaque affecture on peut calculer plein de choses, prendre en compte des tas de conditions et puis ça fonctionne. Mais pour un jeu en temps réel, c'est pas terrible. Les jeux vidéo modernes tournent à 30 ou 60 images par secondes, les fameux FPS. 60 images par secondes, ça veut dire que toute la logique du jeu est réappliquée en boucle environ toutes les 16.000 lisse-gondes. Toutes les 16.000 lisse-gondes, le code du jeu, calculent les graphismes, les animations, les sons, gèrent les contrôles, l'interface et doit donc décider du comportement des édnemis. Si on utilise un arbre de décision pour ça, ça veut dire qu'on doit le repart courir entièrement toutes les 16.000 lisse-gondes. En déjà, ça peut être coûteux en temps de calcul si les arbres sont grands et qu'il y a beaucoup d'entités agirées, mais surtout ça fait qu'il n'y a aucune logique de continuité dans les comportements. Toutes les 16.000 lisse-gondes, notre PSG, va potentiellement faire un truc différent, sans cohérence, sans mémoire de ce qui a été fait avant. Comme s'ils reboutaient son raisonnement 60 fois par seconde. Et pour un jeu en temps réel, c'est assez moyen. Une façon plus efficace dans ce cas, c'est d'utiliser ce qu'on appelle des machines à état fini. C'est un modèle de calcul qui a été étudié en informatique théorique et qui est très utilisé en automatique industriel, par exemple pour gérer le comportement d'un ascenseur. Pour gérer un PNG avec une machine à état fini, on définit un certain nombre d'Etat dans lequel notre personnage peut se trouver. Par exemple, pour un ennemi, il peut être passif, en alerte, en train de nous poursuivre ou bien en train de nous attaquer. L'Etat dans lequel se trouve notre personnage va addicter son comportement. Et puis on définit des règles de transition entre les Etats, quels sont les événements ou les situations, qui peuvent amener le PNG à passer d'un Etat à un autre. Par exemple, si l'ennemi vous vous entend, il peut passer de passif à alerter. Si vous vois, il passe de alerté à poursuite. Et si il est apporté de vous, il vous met en joue, etc. L'intérêt de ce système, c'est qu'il fournit une continuité. Le PNG reste à priori dans l'Etat où il se trouvait avant, sauf si ce passe quelque chose de significatif, qui le pousse à changer d'Etat. Et le code du jeu a juste à déclencher les transitions quand c'est nécessaire. Un jeu vidéo célèbre qui a popularisé ce modèle de comportement, c'est le bon vieux Pac-Man. Les ennemis sont les quatre fantômes et ils ont trois états possibles, en chasse, effrayé, donc en fuite, ou bien neutralisé et donc en déroute. De base, alors ils sont en chasse, si ils essayent de vous choper. Si vous mangez une super PAC-GOM, il passe dans l'Etat effrayé et se met à fuir. Et si vous les croquez, il devienne en déroute et retourne dans leur coin d'origine. Chaque fantôme du jeu a des règles de chasse différentes des autres, mais la machine à Etafini est essentiellement la même. Dans le cas de Pac-Man, c'est vraiment une implementation simple. Mais des tas de jeux ont poussé assez loin l'utilisation de cette technique. On peut ajouter par exemple des transitions probabilistes pour avoir de la variété, surveiller tout un tas de conditions différentes, etc. Le shooter Doom sorti en 1993 a aussi utilisé une version plus sophistiquée des machines à Etafini avec 8 états de base et un certain nombre de sous-condition permettant de gérer tout un tas de variété d'ennemis. Mais c'est certainement le mythique Half-Life 2 sorti en 1998 qui aura poussé le plus loin la méthode des machines à Etafini, en donnant notamment à cette technique des sous-structures à chaque état pour favoriser le réalisme et la finesse des comportements des ennemis. Il faut dire qu'un des défauts de cette méthode c'est qu'elle supporte assez mal la croissance du nombre d'états. Imaginez que vous vouliez prendre en compte la barre de vie du personnage, les armes non-ti-disposes, sa situation par rapport aux joueurs, la visibilité, etc. Et bien ça fait toute une combinatoire de conditions qui conduisent à l'explosion du nombre d'états et donc des transitions entre ces états. Si vous avez NÉTA, vous avez à priori NKR et transition possible et pour les designers et les développeurs ça devient très difficile à lire, à débuguer et à faire évoluer. En plusieurs méthodes ont été imaginées comme les machines à Eta hiérarchique où chaque état principal est lui-même une machine à Eta, mais sans complètement résoudre tous les problèmes. La solution que presque tout le monde utilise aujourd'hui est venue d'un changement de méthode, inspiré là aussi d'algorithmes d'informatique industriel, les arbres de comportement. Dans une publication de 2003, un chercheur en ingénier logiciel, Jeff Dromay, a proposé une nouvelle méthode pour formaliser la façon dont on articule le lien entre les spécifications d'un produit et les comportements qu'il adopte. Dans sa publication, le catifique qu'il illustrait, c'était un four à micronde. Mais la technique a été très rapidement repérée et adaptée d'un côté par les roboticiens et de l'autre par les programméria des jeux vidéo, et notamment les développeurs du jeu Halo 2 sortient en 2005. Un arbre de comportement c'est donc un arbre dont les...

name: Simone Giertz
video_title: I turned your most requested project into a product
transcript: Today is a really big day because I am launching the laundry chair on Kickstarter. It started as a video here on YouTube and I know I said I'm never going to do a Kickstarter campaign again, but here we are and I can stop whenever I want. I just wanted to show you the Kickstarter video because I am so proud of it without much further ado. Here is my pitch as to why you deserve a better bedroom chair. Everybody has this chair covered in clothes that are not clean but also not dirty. The problem isn't laundry, washing clothes too often actually wears them out. The real problem is that we don't have a place for half dirty clothes, so I made this. This is the laundry chair, but it's not just a chair, it's a storage solution. How's that for snazzy Kickstarter language? The real, let's you hang the clothes you're still wearing and when you don't want to look at them, it rotates neatly at a site behind the chair. My name is Simone Yatch. I am an inventor and the founder of Yatch Studio and for the last 10 years, I built everything from shitty robots, which yes, that's the technical term, to custom pickup trucks. I love figuring out how to add functionality to everyday objects, like this coat hanger, the folds in half, so you can add storage to shallow spaces, or this calendar that you can use to track your daily habits. I actually built the first version of this chair for a YouTube video in 2024. Imagine that these are chair legs. There's also a rail like this that you can use to hang your clothes on. Since then, so many of you have reached out asking where you can buy it, but I can't just take your word for it. That's why we're doing Kickstarter. So you can put your money where your thumbs are. Over the last year, we've refined and tweaked the original design, making it lighter, more durable, flat-packable for safe shipping, while keeping the sole the same. It's made from hardwood with premium fabric and a built-in ball bearing system that lets the rail move smoothly and quietly. The laundry chair gives your half-dirty clothes the home they deserve, and a chair you can actually sit in, because I believe that good design should align with how we actually live. So if you have that chair, then this is the upgraded version. Okay, bye.
