The Kingdom of Poland The Polish people are Slavic and migrated to Europe as part of a greater Slavic migration to Europe during the first millennium. They settled along the Varta River and from there the population increased until they settled most of modern day Poland and Western Belarus. These Polish tribes were eventually unified into a single kingdom in the 10th century and through ups and downs Poland grew to become a regional power in Eastern Europe. But this power had a big problem because Poland is located on the European plains. This is a very large but also very flat region of Europe, starting in modern day Germany and extending into Russia. And the flat region makes it easier for armies to march across. And as a result Poland could be attacked from any side at any time with these. There were no mountains to block their routes, very few major rivers, and only the Baltic coast to the north provided any protection. In fact, Poland had been conquered many times throughout its history, ranging from Atela Dahan to the invasions of World War II. And in order to defend such a large open territory, it wasn't possible to just build forts everywhere that would be way too expensive. Instead Poland decided to create a mobile defence by focusing on cavalry, because planes are perfect for horses to graze on. It meant Poland was able to develop one of the best cavalry in human history with all the horses they owned. That cavalry could quickly intercept enemy forces marching into the territory. And that cavalry could be used to attack their neighbours in turn, because it was easy to march into Poland, but it was also easy to march out of Polish lands and into someone else's lands. With Poland eventually conquering all of this territory that you see on screen right now by the 14th century, the Polish cavalry would remain instrumental in this conquest and its defence all the way up to the 20th century. But this also causes a problem. If it's easy to march across Poland with cavalry and you need someone to command your cavalry, then those commanders can turn against the king. But at the same time, if the king commanded the cavalry himself from his capital, then travel times are too long and he wouldn't be able to deploy them anywhere on time. And as a result, the kings of Poland were forced to work together with the nobility and give them what they wanted or else that nobility would turn the Polish army against this king. This caused Poland to create a semi-democratic system, with the king having a parliament called the same. In it, prominent nobles were elected to parliament and ruled Poland along with the king, electing new laws and making sure their voices were heard. While countries like France became absolute monarchies, where the king could do whatever they wanted, the kings of Poland had to get approval from their nobility in parliament. Chapter 2 The Grand Duchy of Lithuania Next, we are going to look at the Lithuanian part of the Polish Lithuanian Commonwealth. Lithuania was in many ways similar to Poland. Living on the European plain, they could be attacked from any direction at any time, just like Poland. But instead of building up a cavalry to defend themselves, Lithuania's solution to this problem was to simply conquer as much territory as possible. After all, your enemies can't conquer you, if you conquer your enemies first, and the more land they conquered, the longer it would take for enemy armies to march all the way into Lithuanian territory and reach the regions and people they actually cared about. The Lithuanians. And as you can see on the map right now, by the 14th century, Lithuania was much larger than it is today. But you will also notice that to the east of Lithuania is Poland. And in the 13th century, the two countries forged an alliance by marrying their royal families to each other. This provided both Poland and Lithuania with at least one border they did not need to worry about. Sometimes they would even support each other in wars in order to keep that one border secure from foreign empires. Because having one ally that they could count on reliably meant that they could focus on defenses elsewhere. As a result, this alliance was incredibly beneficial for both sides and the two ruling families continued to marry one another in order to maintain that alliance by marriage for centuries. This alliance was made permanent in 1385. In that year, the king of Poland died, however, he did not have a son to rule the kingdom after him, but he did have two daughters. And out of the two daughters, Yetviga became queen after a succession crisis. She then offered to marry the grand duke of Lithuania. That would mean that if they had children, then that child would inherit both the title of King of Poland and the title of Grand Duchy of Lithuania, one ruler to countries. And a ruler would never invade himself, and so Poland and Lithuania would never have to worry about going to war with each other. But in exchange for this marriage, the grand duke of Lithuania would have to convert to Catholicism. He agreed, and in 1386 they became co-monarchs of Poland and Lithuania. However, just because they had the same rulers didn't make them the same country. Poland was largely Catholic, while Lithuania was pagan and orthodox. They had different government structures, different tax rates, different laws, and different relationships between the monarch and their vassals. As a result, they remained two very separate countries. But because they did have the same struggles and the same kings, they would often fight side-by-side with each other. A good example was in the early 15th century against the two tonic knights. They were a powerful neighbor of both Lithuania and Poland, so they worked together to defeat the tonic knights in 1410. And over the decades, the two countries would keep fighting alongside each other over and over again, deepening their alliance every time. In a region where everybody invaded everybody else, this alliance provided a rare sense of security, which allowed them to prosper, because this allowed them to focus more on trade and development. With the tonic knights defeated, Poland actually gained direct access to the Baltic Sea, and thus providing them with not one, but two safe borders, Lithuania and the Baltic Sea, because at the time deploying an army across the sea was almost impossible. This sea access also provided them with new trade routes going from the Baltic Sea to the North Sea and all of Western and Northern Europe. With so many rivers in Poland and Lithuania, it became possible for a ship to take cargo, upriver, transported downriver to the seas, and then go up another river in Europe, such as the Rhine and trade with the Dutch, the French, or the Germans. And so trade began to increase. Poland sought mostly industrial goods, while Lithuania sold a lot of raw materials, like timber, wool and grain, while buying steel and steel-based goods from other countries. Over the course of the 15th and early 16th centuries, the centres of trade along the rivers expanded from villages into towns into cities, the nobles who collected taxes on this trade grew rich and powerful, and with most trade going westward towards Western Europe. Lithuania also began adopting more western customs. Catholicism became more popular, and Lithuania eventually adopted their own system of a parliament with a council of lords. Interestingly enough, even though this council diminished the power of the king, the king was actually in favour of this council, that's because the biggest problem of a king ruling two countries was to deal with two systems of government, two militaries, two sets of laws and two of everything. The kings all wanted easier government, and so they were all supporters of the two countries coming closer together, determining that one day, Poland and Lithuania will be united. However, the wealthier they became, the more other states wanted to have their own access to the Baltic Sea and take that wealth for themselves, and in 1547 a new power rose towards the east, that sardom of Russia, and that russian tsar wanted to connect his own territory to the Baltic Sea, and so Russia prepared for war, and in 1558 they launched an invasion of Lithuania in order to get a piece of territory called Livonia, located right here, which is why this war was called the Livonian War. Russia was quite successful at first, and Poland feared that if Russia defeated Lithuania they might one day come for Poland, and could you imagine that a Russian invasion of Poland, and so Poland decided to fight alongside Lithuania for many, many long years. The war lasted so long in fact that the king, called Sigismund II, was growing old, and that was a problem, because Sigismund didn't have any children who could inherit his throne, so while they were fighting the russians, different factions were...

name: The Gravel Institute
video_title: Marianne Williamson: Why You're So Sad
transcript: Tell me one thing. Do you feel sad, or maybe lonely, or depressed, anxious, tired, burned out, full of dread or despair? Maybe you feel alienated from other people, like you're apart or a drift. If so, you are not alone, everyone, practically everyone is feeling the same way. They are the dominant feelings of our time. You've probably heard this described as a mental health crisis, and that's true. Something has gone wrong with our heads, with our mental health. Something is out of whack. Just take a look at the world around us. We live in an age of overwhelming atomization. More and more people report that they feel deeply lonely. Americans, especially young Americans, report having fewer friends and fewer romantic partners or meaningful connections than did people of past generations. Perhaps not coincidentally, rates of depression and anxiety, and of behaviors associated with them are going up. Way up, more people than ever are using antidepressant medications like SSRIs. And more and more people are committing suicide, or considering it. In June 2020, a quarter of young Americans seriously considered committing suicide. Suicides and drug overdoses classified as deaths of despair have become so common that they have driven down American life expectancy from its peak in 2014. By 2021, fentanyl overdose was the most common form of death among Americans aged 18 to 45. All around us, we detect the mood of pessimism, nihilism, self-destruction. Fewer people having children, more people killing themselves, more and more people dying from overdoses, more and more people becoming dependent on pharmaceuticals. Talk to young people or students or really anyone, and it's hard to deny this simple truth. Something is very wrong. But the response to this quote-unquote mental health crisis is a very telling one. Our major institutions from schools, colleges, workplaces, how do they respond? They say that it's something as simple as better work-life balance. They might encourage you to take regular five minute breaks from work, or give yourself off, maybe suggest you up your prescription. They might suggest you push a notification on your phone to let you know if you spent too much time staring at your screen. They do everything except the one thing which is to solve the essential problem. The real problem is that the system itself will keep you complacent and productive enough for its purposes, but the real problem, the depression, the anxiety, the loneliness, that does not go away, because we're treating this as an individual problem and not a social problem. They turn a social problem, a political problem, into a biological one, and while that might make sense on an individual level, it does not make sense from the perspective of a society, because it is not normal for so many people to be this depressed. It is not normal for so many people, especially young people, to be so reliant on antidepressants. It is not normal for them to feel so alone, for them to kill themselves at such high rates, for them to report such high rates of anxiety and loneliness and depression. That is not normal. There is something really wrong. Mental illness, depression, anxiety, these may manifest neurologically, but they are socially and spiritually rooted. If so many people in a society are depressed, if so many people are killing themselves, if so many people are reliant on pills trying to avoid suicide, that says something, something bad about the society in question. We are the lonliest people in human history. There has never been a society as atomized, as alienated, as alone as we are now. It is unprecedented in the hundreds of thousands of years that humans have been on earth for us to be this separated from each other, because the truth is that at a basic level, we are hardwired for connection. We need other people. We find ourselves, our identities, our meaning and our purpose through other people. We are social animals. We have an urge to love and to be loved, to be recognized, to be respected and to belong. We need other people. We need family. We need friends. We need a community. And we live in a society now where for far too many people, those things are impossible. Let's begin with a man named Robert Putnam. A few decades ago, Putnam was a famous professor, a sociologist who studied the trends and changes in American life. And in the 1990s, Putnam was taking a look at what had shifted in America since the 1960s. And as he looked, Putnam began to find something disturbing. American community was falling apart. If you look back at American history, you find something kind of incredible. The level of organic community in the United States, from its founding to just a few decades ago, was remarkably strong. Go back a hundred years and look at the communities around you. Towns and cities in America used to be places with vibrant and thriving communal life. They had labor unions, charity associations, gardening clubs, bridge clubs, churches, scout troops. People went to local parades. They knew their neighbors. They knew what was going on in their community. And that sense of community is what gave meaning to their lives. It's how they met their friends, their spouses. It's where they raised their kids. It's what filled out the contours of their lives. It's what made them happy. You could be an ordinary person living an ordinary life, and you could find dignity and community. A sense of worth in these places. You had a social world all around you. You had a net of support all around you. But by the 1990s, as Putnam was looking at the country around him, he found a social world in decay. Because starting in the 1960s and accelerating ever since then, all these communities and institutions had been collapsing. Church attendance was falling. Local clubs were closing down. Local newspapers were losing readers. Unions were losing members. Families were falling apart. Community was disintegrating. Americans were withdrawing, going inward. They stopped even knowing who their neighbors were. They stopped going to parades or civic events. They stopped being a part of any real local community at all. Even things as simple as local bowling leagues were disappearing. Putnam wrote that Americans had begun in his words, bowling alone. Americans were now spending more and more time without other people. They were expressing lower and lower levels of social trust, not just in their leaders, but in each other. The social fabric of American life was unraveling. And once you understand that basic fact, the decline of organic communities, the last few decades of American life start to make a lot of sense. Because without organic communities around them, people were deprived of the support and comfort and stability that they had once enjoyed, not to mention friendship and love. They regarded each other with more suspicion. Life became zero-sum. Everyone was in it for themselves. And when everyone around you was a stranger, life just becomes about you, about getting what's yours, about becoming a CEO or a billionaire getting out on top and

name: Epic History TV
video_title: Napoleon's Greatest Gamble: Escape from Elba
transcript: February 1850 A small flotilla slips quietly across the Mediterranean, west of Alba. It's most important passenger, a former emperor, now a fugitive, embarked on the greatest gamble of his astonishing career. Napoleon Bonaparte was the greatest general of his age. His armies had once dominated Europe. But just eleven months earlier, at the end of a ten-year conflict that left millions dead. He'd finally been comprehensively, disastrously, defeated. The great powers of Europe had hoped to consign Napoleon to history, giving him the tiny island of Alba to rule. And restoring France's Bobon monarchy. But the emperor of Alba soon became restless, whispers reach him. That France's new king is despised, and many wish for his return. That his enemies, Britain, Russia, Prussia and Austria, have turned on each other and may have plans to move him to a more remote location. Napoleon will be no mere spectator to his own fate. His faith in his own destiny is undiminished. Napoleon will act and roll the dice of fortune once more. He will make a daring bid to reclaim his throne and restore France to glory. Even if it means plunging all Europe into war, once more. But Napoleon's quest may be over before it begins. A day after sailing from Alba as the sun falls over the Mediterranean, Napoleon's vessel passes within shouting distance of Zephyr, a brig of the French king's navy. But for an unknown reason, there is no attempt to question or search the suspicious vessel. Perhaps Napoleon's star of destiny still shines after all. On the 1st of March 1815, Napoleon lands at Golfezouan, with 1,000 grenadiers of his imperial guard. He's just 15 miles from Nice, where his glorious first Italian campaign began 19 years earlier. Napoleon has no illusions of the danger. He must win support and turn the tables on his enemies before they trap and arrest him or worse. Therefore, he must travel fast before they can organize. X, on the main road, is a royalist stronghold. So Napoleon takes a more mountainous route to the east. He and his entourage travel 190 miles in six days, along narrow winding tracks. There is snow and ice and sheer mountain cliffs. Napoleon, now 45 years old, has to travel on foot for much of the journey. Sometimes he stumbles and needs assistance. The contrast to the youthful first consul Bonaparte of 1800 crossing these same mountains is stark. As he moves north, things start to improve. At Cisteron, Napoleon is welcomed by the mayor. At Gap, he is cheered by crowds. But then, on the 7th of March, outside Grenoble, the road is blocked by French troops sent by the king. The 5th line includes many veterans of the empire, men who have served under Napoleon's command. There is a standoff. The emperor's grenadiers implore the 5th to join them. Then Napoleon himself steps forward, throwing open his grey coat. Soldiers of the 5th, if there is one among you who wishes to kill his emperor, he can do so. Here I am. A royalist captain gives the command, fire. There is silence. Then, cries of vive l'empereur ring through the regiment. They are one over. On my march to Grenoble, I was an adventurer. Napoleon later recalled. In Grenoble, I once again became a sovereign. At the court of King Louis XVIII in Paris, there is disquiet, but no great sense of alarm. An outlaw adventurer and his small band of followers are surely no threat to the French state. The men who now draw up a plan to stop this escapade know the fugitive well. Marshal Sult, now minister of war, joined by Marshal's Udi-no, Macdonald and Nay. Men who had won titles, riches and glory under Napoleon, but continue to hold senior roles under the Bourbons. Nay, in particular, whom Napoleon had once dubbed bravest of the brave, seems to have switched his loyalty with zeal. I will seize Bonaparte, he tells the king, and I will bring him to you in an iron cage. Nay marches south with 3,000 troops to arrest his former master, but everywhere there are signs that Bob on authority is collapsing. In Lyon, Napoleon is welcomed with celebrations. Whole regiments are defecting to him. Nay hears grumbling within his own ranks. He's haunted by the prospect of civil war. Napoleon writes directly to Nay. I shall receive you as I did on the morrow of the battle of the Mosqueva, he tells him. Nay is one over. The cause of the Bourbons is lost forever, he tells his troops. Only the Emperor Napoleon is entitled to rule over our beautiful country. There are cheers amongst his men as he orders them to turn around and march on Paris. Nay's defection is the final straw for King Louis. On the 20th of March, he flees the Toulary Palace, heading for safety in the Netherlands. That evening, Napoleon enters Paris. At the Toulary, he is acclaimed by former ministers, dignitaries and soldiers, many of whom are now out of work. Marshall Davout is there to welcome him. He had been ostracized by the Bourbons for his loyalty to Napoleon. Even Joseph Fouche, the wily minister of police for the last 16 years, is on hand to offer his services, which are quickly accepted. In these first weeks in Paris, Napoleon is a well-wind of activity. He appoints new ministers, prefects and mayors. He promises a new liberal constitution on the British model that will limit his powers. He lifts censorship and restarts public works. Whether such measures are long-term commitments or mere populism to win support will never be tested. Bonaparte loyalists across France burst into activity, but many others are more cautious, preferring to wait out events. Because across Europe, there is the confident expectation that Napoleon's return can only mean war.

Well, anyway, that's why Ezio needs to go back to Florence as the party is seeking to overthrow the Medici, and he must stop them from disempowering a family of corrupt bankers that also own slaves, assassins we need to talk. The conspirators, Francesco and Jacobo Bipazzi, Bernardo Barancelli, Stefano Bagnone, Archbishop Salviati and Antonio Maffè, that's all accurate, unlike my pronunciations, however they are shown to all be Templars, abiding by the will of Cardinal Rodrigo Borja, who was real, but also really not involved in this. The stabbing doesn't seem so bad now does it? name: Simple History
video_title: Paratrooper Solutions That Shouldn’t Work (But Do)
transcript: When paratroopers drift down and land in hostile territory, they expect to be surrounded by the enemy. Since they'll almost certainly be outnumbered and outgunned, they had to come up with unique ways to stay alive and fight back. From crazy delivery methods, transporting extra firepower or just gaining a psychological edge with a fancy haircut, they came up with a wide variety of tools to get the job done. The most conventional way paratroopers are deployed is to jump out of an airplane. At the Soviet Union, they had a different idea. In the 1930s, the Red Army was experimenting with deep-battle doctrine, which meant deep and rapid strikes behind enemy lines. And paratroopers were a perfect fit for this strategy. It was vital that the paratroopers all land together and avoid dispersal, so rather than jumping out individually, they tried a different tactic. They would fly in a Tupolev TB3, a bomber converted for troop transport when they reached the drop zone. Rather than simply jump out of a door, they would climb out of the fuselage and shimmy along the wing, using steel cables as hand grips. Once the men were in place, the jump master would give a hand signal and the group would jump, or more accurately slide off the wings, deploying their parachutes as they fell. In theory, everyone would go at the same time, keeping the unit together and avoiding dispersal. During the war, the Red Army was caught off guard by Operation Barbarossa, and most of the paratroopers were thrown into the front as standard infantry. There were some Soviet airborne operations, but these were many years into the war and had limited success. Paratroopers are, by definition, light infantry. The weight and space requirements of airborne operations means they have limited space available. Most did have machine guns, mortars and other heavier weapons, but things like vehicles were not a normal part of their kit. There are some exceptions though. Already in use was the M20 75mm recoilless rifle, a compact direct fire artillery piece. Also readily available was the Vespo 150 scooter, used by civilians as a cheap alternative to a car, especially in France and Italy. Combined the two together and voila, the Vespo 150 tap. Made for the French military in 1956, the platform was lightweight, cheap and easy to use. It is a civilian scooter, so how hard can it really be to drive? They were meant to be deployed in pairs, one to prioritize shooting and the other to carry extra ammunition. The intention was for paratroopers to have a mobile anti-tank capability that can defend against Soviet armor should the cold war turn hot. As strange as it may seem, the Vespo 150 tap actually saw combat and was surprisingly effective. How much damage an M20 can do against a Soviet tank is debatable, but the weapon was more than a match for bunkers, machine gun nests and barricaded rooms. It was used to grid effect during the Algerian war for independence, man was able to navigate the narrow streets of urban battlefields with ease, providing lightly armed paratroopers with the fast, reliable and effective boost in firepower. One of the most pressing concerns for a paratrooper is how to fit their gear in as small a space as possible. Many nations solved this problem by issuing paratroopers with more compact weaponry. Japan solved theirs by giving their paratroopers a full-size rifle that broke in half. The Terra Type 2 paratrooper rifle is almost identical to the standard Type 99 rifle. It was bolt action and chambered in 7.7 by 58mm cartridges that were loaded with 5 round stripper clips. The unique feature was the ability to be broken down into two halves. These could be easily stored in a separate bag for a combat jump, then quickly assembled once the paratrooper was on the ground. It was held in place with a large threaded wedge that held the two pieces together and was large and simple enough that a trained soldier could assemble it in the dark by feel alone. Around 21,000 Type 2 paratrooper rifles were produced during the war and were a key part of the few Japanese airborne operations. One of these was in December 1944 in the Philippines. Japanese paratroopers landed behind American lines and managed to cause some damage, like destroying several spotter planes, but were soon overwhelmed by American firepower. Most surviving Terra Type 2 rifles come from this operation. The weapons taken by the Americans as souvenirs after the fighting was over. Probably the most famous airborne operation was during D-Day or the landing at Normandy. American, British and Polish paratroopers would land before the amphibious invasion and disrupt enemy communications and reinforcements and generally cause havoc behind German lines. Spearheading this was Jake McNeese and the filthy 13 paratroopers that landed hours before the others. Being part Choctaw himself, he and the other members of his unit painted their faces in Native American war paint and shaved their heads into a Mohawk style. Now, this served no practical purpose, but it was a major psychological boost. The men were the first to drop into one of the most important military operations in history. Anyway, they could cite themselves up for battle, would be a massive morale boost. Other paratroopers did similar things like shaving their hair into Mohawks in the days before D-Day. What they didn't know was that the Germans had spread propaganda to the civilian population of France, stating that the inevitable Allied invasion would be led by paratroopers, all of whom were convicted criminals or recruited from insane asylum and could be identified by their nearly shaved heads. When the airborne troops found out about this, they thought it was hilarious. When paratroopers drop, they're cut off from resupply and have to take all of their gear with them. However, they can only carry so much equipment directly. To make sure they have all the supplies they need, paratroopers would not be the only things dropping to the ground. One of these was the British CLE, or Container Light Equipment. There were several different variants, but all had the same basic principle. There were cylinders that could break open in half and be filled with extra ammunition, weapons, rations, radio equipment, and anything else they could need. The CLE could be fixed to the outside of an aircraft on bomb mounts and after release drift to the ground on a parachute deployed from a static line. For the Germans, the drop canisters were not a luxury, but a necessity. Unlike their American and British counterparts whose parachute harnesses had four points of contact at the front, connected to a quick release box, the shoots for the Falscher Mieger had a single point in the center of their back. Instead of landing on their feet, they would land on their hands and knees and would have to perform a summer salt once they hit the ground. This odd style of landing meant they couldn't carry rifles or other weapons and would drop unarmed except for a pistol, knife, and maybe some grenades. Canisters were the only way German paratroopers could actually fight with any effectiveness. There are many limitations of paratroopers and military planners worked tirelessly to solve them, what they came up with, with something as effective as it was obvious, just land the entire plane. Gliders are unpowered aircraft that would be towed by a steel cable to their target by a larger powered plane, like a C-47 or a JU-52. And when they near the landing zone, they would detach and glide the rest of the way. They could arrive silently, landing in an open field without the enemy being any wiser. There are many advantages to using gliders over jumping out of a plane. The troops would all land together, so dispersal is less of an issue, and the gliders could carry heavier weapons and even vehicles and small artillery pieces. They could also land on open ground that was too small for a conventional plane, and there was no risk of losing a more valuable conventional aircraft, landing behind enemy lines. The biggest downside, though, is the lack of control. They were at the mercy of the towing aircraft, and once they were detached, it was all momentum and prayer at that point. The pilot could steer, but an unarmed flimsy fabric and wood aircraft was not the safest of vehicles. Crashes were commonplace, and they could suffer casualties even before they met the enemy. But in spite of their limitations, they played a pivotal role in airborne operations throughout the war. Delivering troops and vehicles behind enemy lines, well before helicopters were developed. These are just a few clever ways paratroopers made the most of their limitations, dropping feet first into legend.

video_title: How did Slavery in the Americas Happen? | Mapped
transcript: Somewhere across the Atlantic, the sea bed became an unmarked resting place for as many as two million people. Men, women, children, thrown overboard, some dead from disease, some still clinging to life. During a voyage so brutal that ships could dispose of bodies in open water and keep sailing as if nothing had happened. Between the early 1500s and the 1860s, approximately 12 to 13 million Africans were loaded onto European ships and transported across the Atlantic Ocean in what became one of the largest forced migrations in human history. 10 to 11 million survived to sea land again. The rest perished during a journey that could last anywhere from six weeks to three months. And for many who survived the crossing, death still came soon after, during the first weeks and months of enslavement or later, after years of forced labor. How did the Atlantic slave trade start and why? Before sugar and before the Caribbean, there was the Portuguese push down Africa's Atlantic coast in the 1400s, an era of ships probing farther each year mapping currents and looking for new areas for gold, spices and trading partners. What was about to come didn't begin as a fully formed master plan to populate the Americas. It began as a series of smaller steps, each one profitable until they stitched together into a system. In the early 1440s, Portuguese expeditions began seizing captives and raids along the west African coast and nearby islands. But raiding was risky, unreliable and it provoked fierce resistance. So the practice shifted. Instead of trying to capture people directly, Europeans increasingly bought captives through coastal trade using local brokers and political authorities who controlled access to the shoreline markets. Systems of slavery and captivity already existed in parts of Africa, though they varied widely from place to place. One moment is often treated as symbolic of that step, becoming a system. It's 1444, a large group of captives is brought to Legos Portugal and publicly sold. An event described by the Portuguese royal chronicler Gomez de Zurara, it wasn't the first slavery and it wasn't the first time Africans were enslaved in Europe. But it was an early, well-documented glimpse of a new Atlantic pattern, voyages, captives, sale, profit and repeat, then came the next step, plantation experiments on Atlantic islands. It all started with a single commodity that most of us take completely for granted today. Sugar Part One, the economics of human cargo. In the 1400s, sugar was a luxury in much of Europe, consumed mainly by elites. They called it white gold, and the name was not an exaggeration. Some even sold it as medicine. Kings presented it as diplomatic gifts, and like any precious commodity in short supply, it created an insatiable demand among those wealthy enough to taste it. But sugar demand alone didn't create a slave system. The missing ingredient was more practical, a new kind of production that required a controllable labor force every day at scale. Europe already had experience with slavery and coerced labor around the Mediterranean, but the Atlantic changed the equation, because these new colonies had land and mills, but not enough workers. In the 1440s, Portuguese voyages began bringing African captives to Portugal. At first, this was not yet a plantation pipeline. Captives could be taken in raids, seized at sea or through deals or even ransomed. They could be used for household labor, displayed as status or sold for profit. One moment could be seen as a symbolic glimpse of where this could lead. In 1444, a large group of captives was brought to Lagos Portugal and publicly sold, described by a Portuguese royal chronicler. It wasn't the first slavery, and it wasn't the first time Africans were enslaved in Europe, but it showed something crucial. A voyage could return with people, and those people could be converted into money, again and again. But raiding was risky and unreliable, and it provoked fierce resistance. So the pattern shifted. Rather than trying to capture people directly in numbers, Portuguese operators increasingly bought captives through coastal trade, using local brokers and political authorities who controlled access to shoreline markets. Trading posts and contracts made the supply more regular than hit or miss raids. Now connects that to sugar. Sugar wasn't just a crop. It was a time-sensitive industrial process. Cut came, crushed quickly, boiled quickly, ship quickly. That required great coordination and long hours, especially at harvest. It pushed investors towards labor they could compel on schedule, not labor that could walk away. This is the point where captives stopped being occasional cargo. Fixed investments in land, mills, boilers, and irrigation only paid off if labor could handle it. Once planters saw that coerced labor could make sugar profitable on Atlantic islands, the model became something they could replicate and export. The Portuguese and other Iberian interests were among the first to scale sugar production in the Atlantic. By the 15th century, they had established sugar plantations on the Atlantic islands off Africa's coast. Madeira, the Canary Islands under Castilian rule, with plantation development influenced by Iberian and Italian capital, and Sao Tomé. These islands were the base of what would become the most profitable agricultural system in human history by that point. The plantation model, large-scale monoculture, intensive labor, maximum extraction of value from both land and workers. By the 1500s, that Atlantic plantation template jumped to the Americas. First, to Portuguese Brazil, where sugar and slavery scaled fast, then across the Caribbean as European rivals fought for land, mills, and markets. In the English Caribbean, the shift became explosive in the 1640s when Barbados pivoted hard into sugar and turned into one of the first fully sugar and slavery economies in the region. This sugar revolution didn't just increase demand. This changed the rules of trade and economy. To protect that investment, colonies began writing slavery into law, hardening it into a permanent, inheritable system. The hours were endless. During harvest season, workers might labor 18 hours a day, seven days a week for months on end. The Caribbean sun was merciless. The conditions were so brutal that plantation owners often treated enslaved labor as replaceable, and in many colonies, mortality remained so high that deaths exceeded births for some periods, and therein lay the crisis. The labor pool was catastrophically insufficient. When Christopher Columbus arrived in Hispaniola in 1492, the indigenous Taino population numbered somewhere between 250,000 and 1 million people. Within 50 years, disease, overwork, and violence had reduced that population to a tiny fraction of its pre-contact size. The same pattern repeated itself across the Caribbean and Brazil with devastating consistency. Smallpox, measles, typhus, and influenza swept through populations that had no immunity to European diseases, even where indigenous people were enslaved and forced to work. The labor supply shrank rapidly through death, flight, and disruption, and planters still demanded a larger, more reliable workforce than the region could provide. The Spanish tried importing European laborers, indentured servants who would work for a set number of years in exchange for passage to the new world. But Europeans also suffered extremely high mortality in many tropical plantation zones, succumbing to tropical diseases like yellow fever and malaria against which they had little acquired resistance. The mortality rates were staggering. Plantation owners needed a labor force that could survive the Caribbean disease environment while enduring the brutal conditions of sugar cultivation, and they found that labor force in Africa. It was partly because many Africans came from regions where malaria and yellow fever were common.

name: Questions d'Histoire
video_title: Comment Giovanni Falcone a décodé les secrets de Cosa Nostra ?
transcript: Palerme, 25 mai 1992. Sous les voutes étouffantes de la cathédrale, une jeune femme de 22 ans, Rosaria, s'avance vers le micro pour pleurer son mari. Dans la voiture, elle a été pulverisée deux jours plus tôt, dans l'attentat orchestré par la mafia qui a coûté la vie au juge falconé à son épouse. Le cri de Rosaria, s'il fallait, déchire l'Italie. Je suis perdue, mais je dois mettre le ginote. Vous avez des courageux de cambiaire. Nous sommes les gens. Cette séquence marque les fondements définitifs du mide de la mafia honorable, qui prétendait par nier les femmes et les enfants. Ce moment de douleur transforme cet attentat en une erreur stratégique fatale pour Cosanostra, nom de la mafia Sicilienne, déclenchant une riposte d'État, qui mènera la capture de la bête, le parent des parents, Tautorina. L'histoire de la mafia est celle d'un état démissionnaire qui a laissé une organisation criminelle remplacée s'éloie par la terreur. De l'unification italienne au guerre mafiause des années 80, Cosanostra s'est infiltré partout en Sicile. Dans les banques, les travaux publics, les services de police, et même au sommet de l'État. Face à ce système sanglant devenu trop écrasant, quelques hommes prétosacrifice se sont battus pour briser le mide de l'invincibilité mafiause. Parmi eux, le plus célèbre, le juge Giovanni Falconn, qui va révolutionner la lutte anti-mafia en suivant l'argent du crime organisé pour le faire tomber. Revenons sur le parcours de ce magistrat qui a osé défié Cosanostra et dont le combat, comme il le savait pertinemment, nous pouvais se solder que par la mort. Avant de revenir sur l'histoire du juge Falcon, laissez-moi vous présenter le sponsor de cette vidéo, Sybergos de VPN. Sybergos de VPN est un logiciel, mais également une application mobile, qui vous permet d'anonymiser votre navigation sur internet, et qui est disponible sur Windows, Mac, iOS, Android et bien d'autres. En masquant votre adresse IP et en cryptant vos données, Sybergos de VPN vous offre un signe sécurité totale, mais aussi d'autres avantages très pratiques, dont le plus intéressant à mes yeux, et le contournement des restrictions géographiques. Avec Sybergos de VPN, vous pouvez changer de localisation en ligne en deux clics et choisir de vous connecter à plus de 11 000 serveurs dans 100 pays. De cette façon, vous pourrez accéder à 40 services de streaming qui ne sont pas disponibles en France, que ce soit Netflix, Prime ou Disney Plus. De plus, si vous êtes fan de sport, vous pouvez changer de localisation et regarder gratuitement les retransmissions en direct de nombreuses événements sur les chaînes locales. Pour vous donner un exemple que j'utilise personnellement, la chaîne PBS propose de nombreux documentaires historiques qui ne sont accessibles que depuis les États-Unis. Avec Sybergos de VPN, vous pouvez dès leur accéder un vaste choix de documentaires en streaming. Sybergos de VPN est très intuitif et simple d'utilisation, et un seul abonnement peut être utilisé par cet appareil, sans qu'il y ait la moindre collecte ou partage de vos données. Sybergos de VPN est disponible sur toutes les plateformes, compte plus de 38 millions d'utilisateurs et à une excellente note sur Toast Pilots. En utilisant le lien que vous trouvez en description et en commentaire, vous obtiendrez 83% de réduction sur un abonnement et quatre mois offert, ce qui revient environ 2 euros par mois. N'hésitez pas à essayer Sybergos de VPN, car vous disposez d'une garantie de 45 jours, satisfait ou remboursé. Un grand merci à Sybergos de VPN de soutenir la création de cette vidéo, et revenons notre sujet. L'assassinat du juge Falcon par la mafia Sicilian. Nous sommes en juillet 1984 à Rome. Dans le bureau ultra sécurisé de Giovanni Falcon, Thomas O. Bouchetta, membre de Cosa Nostra, surnommé le Boss des deux Monde, se tient face à lui. Il vient d'être extradé du Brésil, où il s'était réfugié. Il se s'est condamné. D'autorina, le chef des Corléones, a lancé une purge sanglante contre les autres clans Sicilian, afin de s'entarrer du territoire. Rina ordonnait d'exterminer plusieurs lignés jusqu'au 20e degré de parenté, pour qu'il ne reste plus aucune graine des Fanny Vincu. 16 membres de la famille de Bouchetta ont été exécutés, dont deux de ses fils. Ils passent à table par N de Rina, et non par amour de la justice. Les Bouchetta ne se voient pas comme un traître. Pour lui, c'est la mafia qui a perdu son honneur en devenant une meute d'assassin. Beneficient du statut de reprenty et d'une protection maximale. Il s'apprête à révéler au juge le fonctionnement de l'organisation criminelle. Sa structure, ses méthodes, et surtout, sa grammaire. Pour Falkon, Bouchetta est un professeur de langue, qui le confie en ses intuitions. La mafia n'est pas un état d'âme. Il y a un ensemble de clans autonomes qui se font la guerre pour le contrôle de trafic et de territoire. Mais bel et bien, une structure pyramidal y est rarchisée, à l'organisation quasi-militaire. Bouchetta prend un Falkon, par exemple, que le mot mafia est littéraire, et que le terme utilisé par ses membres est cause à nos strah, notre chose. L'organisation repose sur tout un système, dans lequel policiers, politiciens vereux, magistrats, tueurs ou traficants, se retrouvent réunies autour d'une même recherche de profit et de pouvoir. Tout ce système s'est construit au fil du temps, mais il est possible de précisément donner sa date de naissance. 1812, année de l'abolition du système Féodale en Sicile. À cette époque, le royaume de Sicile est sous protecteur abritanique pour faire face à l'avancée Napoléonée ananitalie. Le royaume unie oblige le royafer d'innan, à abolir le système Féodale vieux de cette siècle, par peur de révolte populaire et pour proposer un contre-model à celui de la révolution française. Ce décrét change tout. Les barons perdent leur pouvoir de justice sur leur terre et s'exilent dans leur palais de palèrement, laissant derrière eux un vie détatique total. C'est dans cette absence de loi que s'installe une nouvelle classe de médiateur, les cabélotis. Ils gèrent et doulent les domaines des barons pour les exploiter à leur compte. Pour maintenir l'ordre et protéger les récoltes, il recueut des gardes armées souvent issus du banditisme, les campieries. Très vite, les protecteurs deviennent les prédateurs. Par la menace, l'insendu des récoltes ou la mutilation du bétail, les cabélotis terrorisent les propriétés rapsons pour les forcer à brader leur terre. Pour légitimer cette violence, il s'appropriait le mid des béatis Paoli, une société secrète médiévale, des justiciés de l'ombre sans s'éproter chez les pauvres contre les puissons. Mais la réalité est moins romantique, une nouvelle forme d'oppression, plus brutal, remplace celle de l'ancien aristocratie. Au fil du 19e siècle, le système se perfectionne et se hiérarchie selon le modèle de la kosca, la feuille d'artichot, les feuilles, les campieries, protège le cœur, le cabélotis. Chaque clompre en le nom de son fief, ou ditoré, malaspina ou Corleon. En 1860, lors du débarquement de Garibaldi, la mafia naissante voit une opportunité. Elle soutient l'unification italienne, espérant devenir un pilier du nouveau régime. Mais les nouveaux dirigeants refusent. Face à un état italien aperçu comme un occupant en lointain, la mafia s'impose comme un gouvernement parallèle. Elle crée l'insécurité pour mieux vendre sa protection. C'est la naissance du pizza, une taxe aurait qu'été à la population. La mafia s'accapare les ressources vitales. La famille Greco contrôle l'eau d'irrigation. D'autres reine sur les agrumins, les abattoirs ou les docs. Le vol de bétail est l'autre activité centrale. Les troupes ont volé puis revendu dans des foires avec deux faux papiers, ou abattu clandestinement pour y nommer les marchiers urbains de viande. Un pacte avec le milieu politique, celle sont impunités. Les cabélotis livrent les votes des paysans à des élus complices, qui garantissent en retour l'accès aux fonds publics et la clémence des juges. En 1865, le mot Mafia, avec deux eff, apparaît pour la première fois sous la plume du préfet de Palermo dans un rapport officiel. Mais ces membres ne s'appellent pas de cette façon. Pour eux, ils font partie d'une non-horable société, et se désigne comme homme d'honneur. La violence franchit un capte symbolique en 1893 avec le meurtre du Marquine au Tarbartolo. Cette ancien mer de Palermo est poignardée de 27 coups de couteaux dans un train. Pour avoir dénoncé la corruption bancaire, lié au député Palizolo. C'est le premier cadavre exquis, le meurtre d'une personnalité, et la preuve que la Mafia infiltrait les sommets de la finance et de l'Etat. À la veille de la grande guerre, l'organisation est partout. Elle pratique l'usure, contrôle la contrebande, et s'apprête à traverser la clantique. L'un obstacle imprévue va bientôt se dresser sur sa route. L'ascension du fascisme et la détermination d'un homme, que l'on amoura, le préfet de faire. En 1924, Benito Mussolini débarquant si s'il est poissoir l'autorité fasciste. Mais lors d'une haute apiana dégréquie, le maire et chef Mafieux donnent Titiokutia comme être un crime de l'aise majestée. Voyons l'escorte du dictateur, il lui glisse avec mes prix. Pourquoi tous ces policiers ? Avec moi vous ne risque rien. Yvre d'orage, le dutchet décide d'écraser l'organisation. Il nomme ses arrêt moris, préfet de palerme, avec un ordre clair. Vous avez carte blanche. Si les lois actuelles vous gènent, nous en ferons des nouvelles. Le préfet de faire engage alors une répression d'une violence inouïe, retournant contre la mafia ses propres armes. En janvier 1926, Mori assièges le bastion de gangue, pour forcer les chefs à ce rendre, il couplo du village, prend les familles en notage, et organise une boucherie assiée l'ouvert en abattant le bataille sur la place publique. En trois ans, Mori procède à plus de 11 000 arrestations. En 1999, Mussolini rappelle Mori à Rome et proclame officiellement la fin de la mafia. C'est une illusion. Si le préfet de faire a décimé les troupes de terrain, il n'a jamais pu toucher les intouchables infiltrés dans les hautes sphères de l'Etat ou du parti fasciste. En réalité, l'honorable société n'est pas morte. Elle s'est mise en sommeil profond, prétareux surgir à la faveur du prochain cas au mondial. En juillet 1943, l'opération Whisky change le destin de la Sicile. Pour préparer l'invasion de l'île, les services secrets américains se licitent le kilotchiano, considérés comme le père du climorganisé model.

name: World War Two
video_title: How To Distract From the Economy - Death of Democracy 10 - Q2 1935
transcript: Berlin, June 30th, 1935, Spartacus also reporting from the German capital. This quarter, Adolf Hitler has played a masterful triple game. He lulled the world into complacency with a grand peace speech, while simultaneously driving a wedge between Western Allies. At home, the Fuhrer used his diplomatic gains on the international stage to distract from the increasingly precarious state of the German economy. Tonight, we look back at a quarter, when Hitler wrapped both foreign leaders and his own people around his little finger to continue his path to rearm Germany and prepare for war. The second quarter of 1935 found the world still under the long shadow of the Great Depression. In Asia, Japan tightened pressure on North China. In Africa, Mussolini's Italy moved ever closer to war with Ethiopia, exposing the fragility of collective security. In Germany, Hitler's successful diplomatic maneuvering to shattered the international opposition won him another victory. The showdown began in early April when Britain, France and Italy formed the Streza Front, driven by mounting fears of German ascendancy. It was a response to the events last quarter when Hitler shocked Europe by openly violating the Versailles Treaty, announcing massive German rearmament and the reintroduction of universal military conscription. Desperate to contain this aggressive military resurgence, the three powers united to condemn unilateral treaty violations and block German expansionism. Their effort was legally and politically anchored by the 1925 Lokano pact, a mutual guarantee for Germany's western border. At Streza, the Allies explicitly pledged to uphold Lokano and protect Austria, hoping this mutual defense network would deter Hitler from moving on the demilitarized Reinland or threatening Austrian integrity. Hitler responded on May 21st with a spectacular address to the Reichstag, now gutted of any importance other than to serve as a stage for the Fuhrer's grandstanding. Germany pledged that Germany needs peace, Germany wants peace, no one of us means to threaten anybody, and issued a direct denial of planning to violate Lokano or have any expansionist designs on his country of birth. Germany neither intends nor wishes to interfere in the internal affairs of Austria to annex Austria or to conclude an Anschluss. He also presented a 13-point peace plan, claimed to renounce war, pledged to uphold the demilitarization of the Reinland, and offered to limit Germany's naval strength to 35% of the British fleet. Universal news services of William Scheirer recorded the impact. Hitler made a grandiose peace speech in the Reichstag this evening. The man is truly a superb orator, tonight he was in great form and his program would convince a lot of people. Desperate to avoid the naval arms race, the British government seized the offer. bypassing the League of Nations and their Streza Allies, British diplomats met with Joachim von Rebentrop in London. On June 18th, they signed the Anglo-German naval agreement along the lines of Hitler's proposal. Domestically, beneath the veneer of diplomatic normalcy, the Nazi party unleashed a new wave of localized anti-Semitic violence, a spring tugged along towards summer storm-groupers and Hitler youth groups, organized riots and boycotts nationwide, under the pretext of punishing race defilement. Nazi moms dragged Jewish men and non-Jewish women through the streets with placards around their necks. In Breslau, the S.A. has been organizing Sunday propaganda processions to publicly denounce non-Jewish women accused of having relations with Jews since earlier this year. In June, the Gestapo began sporting the action by arresting some 20 people, Jewish men and non-Jewish women for race defilement. Here Hitler's high-staggered dress, some Nazi leaders temporarily attempted to curb or at least distance themselves from the worst public excesses to ensure the British naval negotiations succeeded, while in private many of them welcomed the increasing violent pressure on the Jewish community. Sources inside the regime say that Goebbels was mighty unhappy about the diplomatically motivated law-lin violence, reportedly noting at the end of May, when shopping with Magda and the Kovhustendom, once again quite a crowd of Jews, left a sort that out again. Apparently, it was a virtual tug of war inside the Nazi leadership with the likes of Goebbels pressuring Hitler to order an increase of the violence, while other leaders, like Hodor Hesse, occasionally appealed for restraint to avoid disrupting delicate foreign diplomacy. Meanwhile, the oppression was also enforced bureaucratically when in May, the new military law was widely understood and applied to exclude Jews. In the end, there was some restraint, but as soon as the naval agreement was signed on June 18th, the reins were let loose and the violence increased again. Just earlier today, June 30th, Goebbels confirmed the end of the temporary restraint when he actively fueled the new wave of Hitler youth and S.A. violence at a party rally, publicly complaining that Jewelry is once more trying to assert itself today on every street. When he wasn't busy stoking anti-Semitic violence, Goebbels used the party-controlled press to wage a calculated psychological campaign in support of Hitler's foreign affairs efforts. After the British published a white paper in March criticizing German rearmament, the Nazi press was now ordered to manufacture furious outrage, yet, as the Abyssinian crisis escalated, the press was strictly instructed to avoid any friction with Italy. In reporting on domestic issues, the press actively stoked anti-Semitic sentiment, often publishing the names of Germans accused of race defilement to invite public harassment. However, in the weeks of May and June, when the negotiations with the British became critical, even Goebbels temporarily restricted reports of anti-Jewish riots to avoid scuttling the diplomatic efforts. Foreign correspondents also found themselves constrained by censorship. Information was tightly managed so that ordinary Germans received only a steady diet of anti-Semitic hate-mongering, diplomatic triumphs, and assurances of Hitler's profound desire for peace. Absent in the news was the crass reality that the financial strain of rearmament had long caught up with the civilian economy. With a rise for an exchange reserves nearly depleted in the spring of 1935, to prevent a complete collapse of international trade, economics minister Yalma Shacht tightly managed his new plan to ration for concurrency and prioritize military imports. Ordinary citizens paid the price as they faced noticeable shortages. During the mid-1935 period, the German economy experienced stagnant real wage rates, coupled with steeply rising costs of living, particularly for basic foodstuffs, while nominal wages remained largely frozen by the state near their 1932 depression levels, real hourly and weekly wages rates have dropped below 1932 levels with no short-term recovery in sight. At the same time, the cost of everyday necessities spiked dramatically between the summer of 1934 and the summer of 1935. In one Berlin district during this one year period, the cost of fresh fruit rose by 300 percent. Lagoons and leafy greens increased by up to 100 percent and eggs and dairy products climbed by up to 40 percent. Nationwide retail prices for beef increased by 32 percent, veal by 55 percent and mutton by 32 percent. Consequently, these steep price increases led to an actual decline in the average per capita consumption of meat, pork, fat, cheese, and eggs during the year. At it together in mid-1935, the German worker is back to a prosperity or, should I say, lack of prosperity level like in the early days of the depression. The only offset is that less men are out of work, so that despite that they are about as badly off as many of them had been on the dole, it might feel better because at least more people have a job. The other thing distracting from continued lack of consumer prosperity this quarter was that Gugas did what he could to feed the population's nationalist pride through the highly publicized foreign policy victories. And so, the mood within the NSDAP leadership was a mix of euphoric triumph in foreign affairs, fears of economic dissatisfaction and impatience regarding domestic racial policies. Thus signing of the Anglo-German naval agreement in June sent a wave of elation through the upper echelons of the party as a validated Hitler's strategy of bold unilateral action. Most of Gugas reflected this jubilation upon hearing the news on June 18th. As I arrive at the Führer's office, naval agreement just signed in London, Führer very happy, big success for Ribbon Trop and all of us. video_title: The Philosophy of BOJACK HORSEMAN – Wisecrack Edition
transcript:
Hey Wisecrack! Jared here. And today we're talking about the 
happiest show in the Netflix's catalogue BoJack Horseman. With its colorful animation, talking animals, and Hollywood glamour BoJack might look like your
typical happy-go-lucky cartoon but this is far from the case. The show’s light hearted veneer hides a dark and downright depressing reality which is fitting given its focus on the disillusionment
that inevitably comes when you look beneath the surface. BoJack reveals the ugly, narcissistic reality that lies behind the dazzling façade of Hollywoo. BoJack: "Most important thing is you gotta give the people what they want." "Even if it KILLS you." "Even if it empties you out until there's
nothing left to empty." From Mr. Peanutbutter's feigned interest "I wanna talk to you..." "...ERICKA!" to studio executives only focused on money to meaningless award ceremonies that only
reward those with established names. In other words: Charlotte: “Hollywoo’s a real pretty town that’s" "smack on top of all that black tar." "By the time you realize you’re sinking" "it’s too late.” But the show goes far deeper. Hollywoo could be considered a metaphor for
existence in general. Just like the façade of Hollywoo covers up the reality of an ugly, hollow industry the trappings of our daily lives cover up the
meaninglessness of existence itself. This idea, known as “Existential Nihilism” is foundational to “BoJack Horseman.” If life is truly meaningless then how are we supposed to deal with it? Welcome to this Wisecrack Edition on: The philosophy of “BoJack Horseman.” What does it know? Does it know things? Let's find out! But before we dive in This Wisecrack edition is sponsored by Best Fiends and the newest game in the Best Fiends trilogy: Best Fiends Forever It's the same characters and story as before but a totally different kind of game. An addictive clicker adventure where you slap, collect, and level up pretty much everything while trying to get as far as you can as fast as you can. Also, something we really like in edition to making crazy addicting games like this Best fiends gives back. As a supporter of the charity Oceana. Helping protect the worlds oceans. So be sure to check out the new Best Fiends game. It's FREE and you can download it from the link below. Let us know how far you get and thanks again to Best Fiends 
for sponsoring this video. Part 1: Distract yourself A key way to cope with the anguish
of a meaningless world is to simply distract yourself. Naomi Watts: "I just wanted to do something 
light and fun" "to distract me from the deep well of sadness that is my life." Princess Carolyn persistently distracts
herself with her job and her compulsive need to
fix the life of those she cares about. Despite complaining about work dominating her life she can’t actually handle the free time
that comes without it. Lora: "Are you gonna head out soon?" Princess Carolyn: "Where else would I go?" When her agency folds she finally has time for the relationship
she always wanted but still finds a way to get right back to the
Hollywoo grindstone. Princess Carolyn: "I think I'm having an epiphany." "I'M GONNA BE A MANAGER!" Ralph: "Oohh.." Todd, BoJack’s best friend and perpetual mooch is happy as long as he has something to do Todd: "Hooray! A task!" The one time we do see Todd without anything to do he immediately starts to spiral into existential despair. Todd: "I need something to do." "A job or a task or a direction in life." "Do I have a purpose?" before finding Something to take his mind off it. Todd may not have much direction or common sense but he manages to keep himself busy: He composes a space opera builds his own Disneyland and starts a number of businesses. "An all hallow's eve store..." "...in January?" "How fiendishly droll." "Nuhhhhhhhhhhhhhh *crash* Similarly, Mr. Peanutbutter spends his life
in a state of distraction. Though he may seem like a moron BoJack: "He's so stupid, he doesn't realize how miserable he should be." Mr. Peanutbutter is more self aware than most. Mr. Peanutbutter: "The universe is a cruel uncaring void." "The key to being happy isn't the search for meaning." "It's to just keep yourself busy 
with unimportant nonsense." "And eventually..." "...you'll be dead." And then there’s BoJack. BoJack’s whole life is a series of distractions. The way BoJack spends his days is so trivial that the show often makes a joke of not showing us what he’s doing half the time. BoJack: "Yeaaah!" "Two days!" "I can do all those things." *two days later* BoJack: "Oh shit!" "I didn't do any of those things." In fact, in the years since "Horsin' Around" BoJack has been able to drift through life in a near constant state of distraction. Princess Carolyn: "Horsin' Around?" "That ended 11 years ago." BoJack: "Really?" "Where does the time go?" *drinks to forget* But in the first episode BoJack has a “mild anxiety attack” that
sends him to the hospital. The doctor tells him he needs to 
take it easier. Princess Carolyn: "Take it easy!? Are you kidding?" "He doesn't have a job he has no real responsibilities he doesn't do anything but take it easy." The show asks a question commonly directed
at celebrities They have everything, so how could they not be happy? BoJack: "Great house, great career, 'great life' " "That must be why I'm so goddamn happy all the time." This question was addressed by 17th Century philosopher Blaise Pascal who is considered by many to be a 
precursor to the Existentialists. For Pascal, when given too much time to think humans will eventually contemplate
their own insignificance which inevitably bums them out. Or as Pascal puts it: “I have discovered that all the unhappiness
of men arises from one single fact: that they cannot stay quietly in their own chamber. I have found that there is one very real reason namely, the natural poverty of our feeble
and mortal condition so miserable that nothing
can comfort us when we think of it closely.” For Pascal, humans have a natural psychological
defense mechanism that keeps these thoughts at bay: We’re really easily distracted. And when we’re distracted we stop thinking about our existence and can be, if not happy at least functional. Pascal directly addressed the
unhappy celebrity question though he uses kings instead of movie stars: “Yet, when we imagine a king attended with
every pleasure he can feel if he be without diversion and be left to consider 
and reflect on what he is this feeble happiness will not sustain him.” Like a king BoJack has no serious struggle to occupy his life. Instead, he has to spend all of his time trying
to distract himself so that he never has to think about the
reality of his situation. Although he succeeds in doing this for years the whole charade comes crashing down when he reads Diane’s book “One Trick Pony.” In the book, BoJack sees an honest reflection of himself warts and all. He is forced to face all the things he really is and isn’t and to confront the extent of his self-deception. "I love the part about how you became famous." "You could be constantly surrounded by distraction" "and wouldn't have to be alone with yourself." So what should BoJack do 
now that his distraction has crumbled? For Pascal, once you’ve passed the critical point where diversion will no longer sustain you the only recourse is to turn to God. But for BoJack this isn’t an option. It seems that Hollywoo is a post-God-Is-Dead world. In the Christmas Special, the show uses Santa Claus 
as a stand-in to discuss the non-existence of God: BoJack: “He’s a lie that grown ups made up because" "we like to believe that there’s an order to the universe" "and that good behavior will lead to happiness" "but the fact is" "that just isn’t true.” But if there’s no God where are we supposed to turn? For BoJack, the first choice is
right back to distraction. The show provides two separate
case studies of what happens when you try to force distraction after the illusion has been broken and neither ends well. First, Secretariat gives a young BoJack advice
that basically amounts to “Don’t Think About It.”
*same as Rick Sanchez* Secretariat: "BoJack." "When you get sad...you run..." "straight ahead" "and..and you keep running forward" "no matter what." "There are people in your life
who are gonna try to hold you back" "slow you down but you don't let them." "Don't you stop running and don't you ever look behind you." "There's nothing for you behind you." "All that exists is what's ahead." But once he’s disqualified from racing and
can no longer keep the thoughts at bay... "You can be the fastest runner in the world, but you can't outrun the truth." The second case is Sarah Lynn. BoJack drags Sarah Lynn on a months long bender that only ends when she OD’s on well BoJack (the heroin, not the horse). Looking back on it that might have been a bit on the nose. If trying to reclaim his distracted state
is doomed to end in tragedy what other options does BoJack have? Part 2: Radical Freedom From the beginning, BoJack rejects responsibility: “I’m responsible for my own happiness?" "I can’t even be responsible for my own breakfast.” He wants to escape the realities
of his own terrible life choices. BoJack: "I didn't do anything wrong because I can't do anything wrong." "EVERYTHING IS MEANINGLESS!" "NOTHING I DO HAS CONSEQUENCE!" After his first bender, BoJack asks Diane: "Am I just doomed to be the person that I am?" "The person person in that book? "I know that I can be selfish and narcissistic
and self destructive" "but underneath all that, deep down" "I'm a good person and
I need you to tell me that I'm good." BoJack is afraid of what 20th Century French
philosopher Jean-Paul Sartre called “Radical Freedom” which was a major focus of his philosophy Existentialism. BoJack: "I stand by my critique of 'Sartre'." "His philosophical arguments helped many 
tyrannical regimes justify over-cruelty." "Also the French smell and I hate them." If Existentialism had a catchphrase, it would be: “Existence Precedes Essence.” An object’s essence, for Sartre, can be thought of as its purpose. If I make a hammer, I create it with a particular purpose in mind i.e. hammering, so we would say that the hammer’s essence precedes its existence. For a Christian a human is like a hammer a tool created by God with a purpose in mind. But for an Existential Nihilist, humans, and horsemen exist just because. Unlike a hammer or a Gentle Farms chicken there’s no purpose underlying BoJack’s existence and nothing he is meant to do. There is nothing stopping BoJack from stealing
the D from the Hollywood sign or doing three months worth of drugs, and on the flip side nothing forcing him to do those things either. BoJack has total control over every choice he makes. This is what Sartre meant by “Radical Freedom” and it's definitely not supposed to be comforting: “That is what I mean when I say that man
is condemned to be free." "Condemned, because he did not create himself," "yet is nevertheless at liberty," "and from the moment that he is thrown into this world" "he is responsible for EVERYTHING he does.” Or, as Diane and BoJack put it: Diane: “That’s the thing, I don’t think I believe in 'deep down'." "I kinda think all you are is 
just the things that you do.” BoJack: "Well that's depressing." There is no “good BoJack” deep down. There is only the one BoJack and he is entirely defined by the choices
he's made so far. Or, as Todd puts it to BoJack at the end of season 3: "You are all the things that are wrong with you." While this might not seem too comforting it does offer BoJack one bit of hope: Yes, BoJack is defined by the choices
he's made so far but he is not “doomed to be the person in that book” forever. He has the freedom to change his actions and himself. But it’s entirely up to him to do so. the truth becomes public." While these qualities are admirable and traditionally
applicable to the good guy in a fantasy series, in the world of Game of Thrones, they act as a detriment and a weakness. It was Ned's positive attributes that led to his downfall, not his negative ones. No salvation came to
justify these qualities, he simply dies. This is largely due to
Martin's affection for history. He has said the books are loosely based on the English War of Roses, and he wanted to show that in war, the truth is that all people, good or bad, have an equal possibility of perishing. He rarely betrays this desire

Again, Martin defied expectations by subverting the genre. He placed Rob in the spotlight, making him and his just campaign one of the primary foci. Rob attempted to take on
several qualities of his father, making an effort to be an
honorable leader worth following. This made the audience
comfortable and content, luring them into a false sense of security where they believed the narrative was back on the traditional path of most fantasy series. At least until the King of
the North's untimely demise. And unsurprisingly, everyone lost it. But despite these initial reactions, the viewership increased. The season 3 finale had
5.39 million viewers where the season 4 finale had 7.09. A similar trend occurred

It makes plenty of sense when you consider how well trained television audiences have become to traditional narratives. They want it to keep character arcs unexpected and honest without betraying the integrity of the story. And that's what Game of Thrones did. By sacrificing so many
early on in the narrative, Martin effectively brought
every character down to the same level. If no character is more

accompany each episode, leaving the audience uncertain as to where and how it will end. Where other series and
writers create the illusion of danger and finality, Game of Thrones makes

transcript: Filmmaking is an insane thing to do. You're working with dozens of people who are experts at their crafts, and a collection of actors playing pretend to create a fake reality by filming hundreds of little pieces of a story out of order that you then have to assemble in a computer later. It's stupidly expensive, it's ridiculously stressful, and it's also kind of the best thing ever. So, I made a movie. As in, one with a real budget and a cast of extremely legit actors and a crew of, I mean, look how many people we have in the credits. It's called The Dinner Plan. We shot it in late 2024, released it on Nebula in late 2025, and that's it right there. Also, this feels like I'm in a junket interview, just one with less flattering lighting. So, I've been making different kinds of film things for years. YouTube videos and short films, and a micro-micro-budget feature film, shout out to all the charl heads over there. And on everything I've ever made, there has basically been no budget and no crew. So, every time I have been the director, but I've also been the cinematographer, the editor, the gaffer, the sound mixer, the VFX artist, the costume designer, and sometimes even an actor. And look, I don't choose to work that way because I think it's more fun or it makes the movie better. I do it because there is simply no other option. But the goal, obviously, has always been to make projects where we have the resources to hire skilled professionals to do all those other jobs so that I can just direct, just focus on that one job. And The Dinner Plan was exactly that, a way to level up and make something on a way bigger scale than I ever have before. Now, I wanted to make this movie for two reasons. Reason one, obviously, just to make a cool, fun movie. And reason number two was to learn how a production like this works, how to work with a professional cast and crew and navigate this different world. I wanted to do this on a short film first before fully jumping into a feature so that when I get to that, I feel prepared. Even though this was on a bigger scale than anything I've made before, a lot of the same rules and ideas apply as when you're making a no-budget film. Like, it's still a movie, so cinematic language is still the same. And on the simplest level, it's still all just a matter of getting the shots you need to tell the story. But the process of getting those shots is pretty different. So now having been through this, actually having been through it twice because I directed another thing last summer which I'll talk about later, I wanted to share the major lessons I learned, lessons that I'm going to take with me into every future project. Also, by the way, this video is sponsored by Nebula where you can go watch the dinner plan right now. There are going to be spoilers for it. So if you haven't seen it, I would recommend clicking the link in the description, signing up now, and you can get a three-day free trial. So go watch it for free. Okay, lesson number one, money equals time. Yes, obviously time is money is an expression that has existed forever. It's a cliche that we all know, but it has a unique meaning when it comes to film. There is so much discussion about money in regards to cinema. We're all obsessed with what movies cost and how much they make. Cinema is other than maybe video games, the most expensive artistic medium there is. Money can buy you famous actors and visual effects and locations, but the biggest, most important thing that money buys you is just time to shoot the movie. See, the reality is that you can only ever get so many shots in a day, and so more days equals more shots. More money equals more time, equals more shots to tell the story. We were originally only going to have four days of production on the dinner plan, which has a 30 page script. So I was really worried about that not being enough time and basically begged the producers for a fifth day. And to their credit, shout out to everyone at Nebula.
